paper_title: Visual Generation in the New Era: An Evolution from Atomic Mapping to Agentic World Modeling

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent visual generation models have made major progress in photorealism, typography, instruction following, and interactive editing, yet they still struggle with spatial reasoning, persistent state, long-horizon consistency, and causal understanding. We argue that the field should move beyond appearance synthesis toward intelligent visual generation: plausible visuals grounded in structure, dynamics, domain knowledge, and causal relations. To frame this shift, we introduce a five-level taxonomy: Atomic Generation, Conditional Generation, In-Context Generation, Agentic Generation, and World-Modeling Generation, progressing from passive renderers to interactive, agentic, world-aware generators. We analyze key technical drivers, including flow matching, unified understanding-and-generation models, improved visual representations, post-training, reward modeling, data curation, synthetic data distillation, and sampling acceleration. We further show that current evaluations often overestimate progress by emphasizing perceptual quality while missing structural, temporal, and causal failures. By combining benchmark review, in-the-wild stress tests, and expert-constrained case studies, this roadmap offers a capability-centered lens for understanding, evaluating, and advancing the next generation of intelligent visual generation systems.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Visual Generation in the New Era: An Evolution from Atomic Mapping to Agentic World Modeling Keming Wu1,12,(cid:242) , Zuhao Yang2,12,(cid:242) , Kaichen Zhang3,12, , Shizun Wang4,, Haowei Zhu1,, Sicong Leng2, Zhongyu Yang2, Qijie Wang1, Sudong Wang11 , Ziting Wang6, Zili Wang6, Hui Zhang9, Haonan Wang4, Hang Zhou8 , Yifan Pu1 , Xingxuan Li7 , Fangneng Zhan10, Bo Li2,12, , Lidong Bing7, Yuxin Song8,, Ziwei Liu2,12, , Wenhu Chen5, , Jingdong Wang8, , Xinchao Wang4, , Xiaojuan Qi3,, Shijian Lu2, , Bin Wang1, (cid:242) Project Organizer. Core Contributor. Senior Supervisor. 1Tsinghua University, 2Nanyang Technological University, 3University of Affiliations: Hong Kong, 4National University of Singapore, 5University of Waterloo, 6StepFun, 7MiroMind, 8Baidu, 9Fudan University, 10Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, 11Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou), 12LMMs-Lab Recent visual generation models have improved photorealism, typography, instruction following, and interactive editing, yet they raise deeper question: what does it mean to become better? Despite high fidelity and complex prompt following, systems still struggle with spatial reasoning, persistent state, long-horizon consistency, and causal understandingexcelling at appearance while falling short of structural, temporal, and causal coherence. We argue the field must move beyond appearance synthesis to intelligent visual generation: plausible visuals grounded in structure, dynamics, domain knowledge, and causal relations. We formalize this as progression from single-pass rendering to controllable composition, persistent coherence, closed-loop interaction, and causal world modeling. We introduce five-level taxonomyAtomic Generation, Conditional Generation, In-Context Generation, Agentic Generation, and World-Modeling Generationfrom passive renderers to interactive, agentic, worldaware generators, where each level nests prior capabilities and adds qualitatively new competence. We analyze drivers of this shiftdiffusion-to-flow matching, unified understanding-and-generation models, improved visual representations, supervised fine-tuning and preference-based post-training, reward modeling, large-scale data curation and synthetic data distillation, and sampling acceleration and distillation for real-time deploymentas collectively advancing shared trajectory toward intelligent visual generation rather than isolated improvements. Evaluations often overestimate progress by privileging perceptual quality over structural, temporal, and causal weaknesses. We therefore pair benchmark review with in-the-wild stress tests and expert-constrained visual case studies that map failure modes to taxonomy levels to locate the frontier. Reframing generation as path toward broader visual intelligence, this roadmap outlines research agenda for controllable, interactive, physically grounded systems. We hope this roadmap provides the community with capability-centered lens and practical insights for understanding, evaluating, and advancing the next generation of intelligent visual generation systems. sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Key Questions ❶ Capability Taxonomy: What does it mean for visual generation model to become more intelligent, and how can we organize progress from atomic rendering to world-modeling generation? ❷ Modeling Mechanisms: How do diffusion, flow matching, autoregressive modeling, hybrid ARdiffusion systems, and unified multimodal architectures each change the trade-off between fidelity, controllability, reasoning, and efficiency? ❸ Training and Data Engines: Why are modern gains increasingly driven by data density, VLM-based relabeling, continued training, SFT, preference alignment, reward models, and acceleration rather than by parameter scaling alone? ❹ Applications as Constraints: How do applications such as personalization, layout control, typography, editing, domain adaptation, and embodied prediction reveal increasingly explicit requirements for structure, memory, and state consistency? ❺ Evaluation and Stress Testing: Why do current metrics overestimate progress, and how can in-the-wild stress tests expose failures in spatial logic, physical reasoning, identity preservation, text fidelity, and causal grounding? ❻ Agentic and World-Modeling Frontiers: What separates todays strong renderers from closed-loop visual agents and playable world models, and how might visual chain-of-thought, tool use, verification, and world simulation define the next stage? The Paradigm Shift: From Static Generation to World Simulation. Visual generation has evolved from narrowly defined text-to-image problem into much broader interface for composing, editing, and increasingly simulating visual worlds. Early milestones in generative modeling established the feasibility of mapping language or latent codes to photorealistic pixels, and latent diffusion systems such as Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al., 2022) made high-quality synthesis scalable and widely accessible. Yet this classical framing largely treated image generation as one-shot distribution-matching problem: given prompt, sample an image that looks plausible. In the latest generation of foundation models, this perspective is no longer sufficient. Rectified-flow and flow-matching objectives (Liu et al., 2022b; Lipman et al., 2023), transformer-native generators such as MM-DiT (Esser et al., 2024), and increasingly unified multimodal systems have transformed generation from passive rendering into controllable process that can absorb reference images, preserve identity, reason over structure, and participate in downstream decision loops. The central question, therefore, is no longer only how to produce sharper pixels, but how to measure and improve the intelligence of visual models: their ability to obey constraints, maintain long-range consistency, close the loop with feedback, and ultimately approximate causal dynamics. This shift motivates new lens for the field, one that treats visual generation not merely as aesthetic synthesis, but as stepping stone toward agentic visual intelligence and world simulation. The Nano Banana & GPT-Image Era: New Capabilities and New Challenges. Frontier systems exemplified by Nano Banana, GPT-Image, and strong open counterparts such as Qwen-Image and Z-Image (Wu et al., 2025a; Cai et al., 2025) illustrate why this new lens is necessary. On the capability side, modern models exhibit far stronger long-prompt comprehension, typography, instruction following, high-resolution fidelity, reference-based editing, and fluid transitions between generation and manipulation. At the same time, unified understanding-and-generation frameworks such as X-Omni, BLIP3o-NEXT, and UAE indicate that visual models are beginning to operate over shared multimodal space in which perception, reasoning, and rendering increasingly inform one another (Geng et al., 2025c; Chen et al., 2025c; Yan et al., Note: If you identify your own or other papers relevant to this roadmap that have not been discussed (we apologize for any such omissions due to the rapidly expanding literature), please feel free to contact us via email or raise an issue on GitHub. 5 2025). Their training pipelines have also changed qualitatively: success now depends not only on scale, but on active data curation, VLM-driven relabeling, synthetic data distillation, and post-training alignment via DPO, GRPO, and learned reward models (Liu et al., 2025d). However, these gains coexist with persistent blind spots. Models that excel at photorealism still fail on puzzle-like spatial reconstruction, multi-step state-transition editing, persistent character identity, physical reasoning, and embodied predictive tasks. In other words, current systems often achieve strong semantic plausibility without robust causal competence. This mismatch explains why conventional metrics such as FID, CLIP-based alignment, or isolated benchmark averages are increasingly insufficient: the field now needs richer task-specific evaluation and in-the-wild stress testing to reveal where present-day models remain confined to statistical correlation rather than genuine visual reasoning. Figure 1 Publication trend of the 411 post-2014 references analyzed. Annual publication count (bars) and cumulative total (line) are computed from the updated bibliography after truncating entries before 2015. The curve reveals an exponential acceleration since 2022, with 2025 alone contributing 188 papers (45.7%). This surge reflects the rapid maturation of visual generation research driven by diffusion transformers, unified multimodal models, and post-training alignment techniques. Scope and Contributions. This work focuses on the rapidly evolving landscape of modern visual generation and editing in the foundation-model era. Rather than cataloging works only by architecture family or application label, we organize the literature around the broader transition from atomic mapping to agentic intelligence. Our goal is to explain not only what has improved, but also what kind of capability is being improved as visual systems move from one-shot rendering toward controllable, persistent, interactive, and physically grounded generation. The main contributions of this roadmap are as follows: We propose capability-oriented five-level taxonomy of visual intelligence, spanning Atomic Generation, Conditional Generation, In-Context Generation, Agentic Generation, and World-Modeling Generation, to clarify what it means for visual model to become more intelligent. We synthesize the major technical drivers behind this evolution, including the transition from diffusion to flow matching, the rise of unified understanding-and-generation systems, the redesign of visual representations, and the importance of pre-training, post-training alignment, reward modeling, and efficiency engineering. We organize the expanding application space into coherent progression from conditional generation and domain-specific adaptation to reasoning-driven editing and embodied interaction, highlighting how practical tasks increasingly demand structure, memory, state tracking, and closed-loop control. 6 We complement benchmark-based review with stress-testing perspective, using real-world case studies to show why high aesthetic quality does not imply mastery of spatial logic, physical consistency, or long-horizon visual reasoning. Together, these perspectives aim to answer the motivating question of this roadmap: what design principles underlie the surprising capabilities of modern Nano Bananaand GPT-Image-style systems, and what still separates them from true playable world models? To ground the scope and timeliness of this work, we present two complementary analyses of the analyzed references. Figure 1 summarizes the post-2014 publication timeline, revealing an exponential acceleration since 2022 with 2025 alone contributing nearly half of the modern cited works. Figure 2 maps research categories by maturity and recency, highlighting the fields ongoing pivot from foundational modeling toward application-driven and alignment-oriented research. Figure 2 Modern research landscape: topic maturity vs. recency. Each bubble represents research category whose mean publication year falls in the modern visual-generation period, positioned by its mean publication year (x-axis) and an adjusted 20252026 recency share (y-axis), with bubble size proportional to paper count. The recency score is smoothed toward the corpus-level average so that very small emerging topics are not artificially pinned to 1.0. Topics in the upper-right quadrant (e.g., image editing, unified multimodal models, world modeling, and agentic generation) are both recent and rapidly growing, while comparatively mature modern foundations such as diffusion & flow models anchor the lower-left. Roadmap of the Paper. The remainder of this work is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces our five-level taxonomy of visual intelligence. Section 3 reviews model foundations, architectural components, and cross-cutting methodological trends, with particular emphasis on the diffusion-to-flow-matching transition and unified understanding-generation systems. Section 4 covers pre-training, post-training, and inference acceleration. Section 5 summarizes datasets, data-construction methodologies, benchmarks, and supporting infrastructure. Section 6 covers major application regimes and evolving frontiers, including controllable generation, domain adaptation, editing, and embodied settings. We then move beyond standard benchmarks to analyze real-world stress tests that expose failure modes in spatial structuring, physical reasoning, and identity consistency, before discussing future directions in Section 8 and concluding in Section 9.

sections.title: 2 The Evolution of Visual Intelligence
sections.content: Overview: The Hierarchy of Generative Capabilities. As foundation models move visual generation beyond isolated image synthesis, it becomes increasingly important to articulate what capability is being improved when we claim model is more intelligent. Inspired by OpenAIs staged roadmap for general AI, we propose domain-specific 5-Level Taxonomy for Visual Intelligence that characterizes models by the type of competence they exhibit in generation, rather than by architecture or task label. Why leveled taxonomy? The five levels reflect nested expansion: each subsumes its predecessors and adds one qualitatively new competence. Two operational axes separate them. How much single forward pass can absorb: L1 takes only prompt; L2 adds one explicit condition; L3 absorbs rich context within one pass. Whether the system orchestrates multiple calls: L4 introduces an external controller that orchestrates multiple passes; L5 further requires that generation be anchored in physical and causal world knowledge. Each level is made precise below and summarized in Table 1. Progress along this taxonomy is not one-dimensional: it is transition from passive statistical rendering to goal-directed, physically informed visual intelligence. This clarifies why many photorealistic systems still fail at persistent identity, long-horizon reasoning, or real-world interactionthey remain at lower capability levels. The taxonomy threads through this work: Sections 3 to 6 can each be read as mechanisms pushing models along this trajectory; Section 7 stress-tests each level with real-world cases; Section 8 discusses what separates the current frontier from L5 simulation. See Figure 3 and Table 1. Figure 3 Overview of the five-level taxonomy of visual intelligence: progression and concrete examples. The figure unifies an abstract progression strip (top, with capability axes Controllability, Contextual Coherence, Closed-loop Agency, and Causal Grounding) with one concrete input/output example per level (bottom). L1: atomic text-to-image generation. L2: single-condition controlled generation. L3: multi-reference and multi-condition composition in one forward pass. L4: agentic generation with plannerrenderverify loop. L5: world-modeling generation respecting physical and domain rules. The five-level structure is thread that runs through this surveysee Table 1 for one-line definitions, the operational distinction between L3 and L4, representative methods, and key challenges.

sections.title: 2.1 Level 1: Atomic Generation
sections.content: Definition. Models at this level perform direct, one-shot mapping from an input conditiontypically text promptto visual output. Their role is to approximate the training distribution and produce visually convincing appearances, rather than to maintain precise structure, memory, or causal consistency. Each generation is independent. Representative Methods. On the diffusion side, DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) introduced iterative denoising rivaling GAN samples without adversarial-training instability; Latent Diffusion Models (LDM / Stable Diffusion) (Rombach et al., 2022) scaled it via compressed latent space; DiT (Peebles and Xie, 2023) replaced the U-Net with transformer that scales reliably with compute. On the autoregressive side, DALL-E (Ramesh et al., 2021), LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024), and VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) show that next-token / next-scale prediction also yields strong one-shot images. Earlier GAN systems (e.g., StackGAN (Zhang et al., 2017)) established prompt-to-pixel feasibility but at limited resolution. Key Challenge. The central limitation of L1 is uncontrolled variation: the model generates what looks right according to the learned distribution, but the user has no mechanism to specify precise spatial layout, object count, identity, or structural constraints. prompt like red cube to the left of blue sphere may produce visually pleasing image where the spatial relation is violated, because the model optimizes for distributional plausibility rather than compositional correctness. We stress-test this failure mode in Section 7, Dimension I, where jigsaw reconstruction tasks reveal that current models default to semantic hallucination rather than geometric reasoning (Section 7.2.1).

sections.title: 2.2 Level 2: Conditional Generation
sections.content: Definition. This level introduces explicit structural or multimodal constraintssuch as depth maps, edge sketches, segmentation layouts, reference images, or identity embeddingsinto the generative process. The model moves beyond unconstrained sampling and becomes controllable visual constructor that can faithfully translate prompts, controls, and references into coherent visual results. While the output now respects user-specified conditions, each generation remains single isolated transaction: the model has no mechanism to carry forward state or memory from one generation to the next. Representative Methods. ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023a) injects spatial conditions (edges, depth, pose) into frozen diffusion backbone via trainable copy. IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023) and InstantID (Wang et al., 2024a) inject reference-image features through cross-attention for identity control; DreamBooth (Ruiz et al., 2023) and Textual Inversion (Gal et al., 2022) extend the models concept vocabulary from few reference images. Layout-to-image methods like GLIGEN (Li et al., 2023b) and CreatiLayout (Zhang et al., 2025a) take bounding boxes or region semantics as input. On the architecture side, SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) builds L2 conditioning into the backbone via MM-DiT. The shared property: the output is constrained sample satisfying user-specified conditions, not free sample. Key Challenge. The central challenge at L2 is spatial precision and attribute binding: ensuring that the model places the right content in the right region with the right attributes, rather than merely producing an image globally consistent with the prompt. Even with explicit layout constraints (e.g., left box: red hat; right box: blue scarf), current models frequently exhibit attribute leakage (the scarf inherits the hats color) or spatial confusion (left-right reversal). We examine these failure modes in detail in Section 7, Dimension I.

sections.title: 2.3 Level 3: In-Context Generation
sections.content: Definition. L3 is still single forward pass, but one that absorbs rich context: multiple references, multiple conditions, or an accumulated history of prior generations and edits. The model has no external controller memory is implicit in the input. This framing reveals why multi-turn editing belongs at L3, not at L4: each round is single forward pass over growing input sequence (image0, edit1, image1, edit2, . . . ), with the model producing imagek in one shot. The challenge is analogous to the difference between answering single question (L2) and sustaining coherent multi-turn conversation where earlier statements constrain later onesstill one model, one forward pass, just with more context. Representative Methods. L3 capability appears in three forms, all sharing the operational signature: one 9 forward pass over rich input. (i) Multi-reference / multi-condition composition. The model fuses several references (subject, outfit, scene) and structural conditions (depth, pose) into one coherent outputthe regime that recent unified backbones target most directly. (ii) Multi-turn editing with cumulative context. SEED-Data-Edit (Ge et al., 2024) and ImgEdit (Ye et al., 2025b) let users chain successive edits (change background, then add sunglasses, then shorten hair); each turn is one forward pass over the accumulated history, requiring pixel-level fidelity in unedited regionsa property favoring VAE-based representations over purely semantic encoders. Cross-panel storytelling extends this: StoryMaker (Zhou et al., 2024d) and Visual Persona (Nam et al., 2025) keep character identity stable across panels via multi-pose supervision and full-body identity representations. (iii) Reasoning trace inside one pass. ReasonGen-R1 (Zhang et al., 2025f) and T2I-R1 (Jiang et al., 2025b) interleave textual reasoning trace with image generation inside single AR sequencestill one forward pass, with no external controller deciding when to stop, placing them at L3. Key Challenge. The defining difficulty at L3 is coherence preservation under cumulative context: the model must update what the user asks (expression, pose, background) while keeping everything else pixel-faithful as the input context grows. Errors accumulatea small identity drift in one editing step compounds across subsequent steps, and even VAE reconstruction error becomes visible after several round-trips. We examine these failure modes in Section 7, Dimension IV, and the specific case of cross-frame identity preservation in Section 7.3.5. Method vs. probe. The L3 classification above is about method: naive multi-turn system stays at L3 because each round is one forward pass with no controller deciding what to verify or roll back. The same task surface, however, is the cleanest probe for L4 capability: system that detects mid-sequence identity drift and silently restores, or triggers re-rendering on context conflict, exhibits L4 even though mechanically each round is still one forward pass. Section 7, Dimension IV uses this dual nature as its probe design.

sections.title: 2.4 Level 4: Agentic Generation
sections.content: Definition. At L4, generation becomes one action inside control loop rather than the final output. Following the LLM literature (Erik S. and Zhang, 2024), we use agentic in the strong sense: system qualifies as L4 when it (i) dynamically decides the next action based on observations rather than following pre-scheduled pipeline, (ii) operates inside an environment feedback loop where outputs change state and the new state shapes subsequent inputs, (iii) persists toward long-horizon goal across multiple forward passes, and (iv) decides when to terminate. The L3-vs-L4 boundary is therefore about autonomy over the trajectory, not the number of user turns. Representative Methods. Following Anthropics distinction between workflows (LLMs and tools orchestrated through pre-defined code paths) and agents (LLMs that dynamically direct their own processes and tool use) (Erik S. and Zhang, 2024), current L4-related systems span spectrum. Workflow side. GEMS (He et al., 2026) composes fixed plannerdecomposerverifierrefiner loop with trajectory-level memory; Gen-Searcher (Feng et al., 2026) extends it with web search and evidence collection. Verification and retrieval become first-class building blocks, but the control flow is engineered, not chosen by the model. Agent side. JarvisArt (Lin et al., 2025) dynamically selects from 200+ Lightroom tools based on user intent and intermediate state. CoT-VLA (Zhao et al., 2025) and UniPi (Du et al., 2023) are the embodied analog: the model predicts visual states conditioned on actions and uses these predictions to choose subsequent actions in robotic policy loop. Most current visual L4 systems sit on the workflow end; full agentic systemswhere the model chooses its own actions toward long-horizon goal in single closed loopremain rare. Two boundary clarifications: (a) unified multimodal backbones (Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2024a), BAGEL (Deng et al., 2025), OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025)) are not L4 agents themselves but provide the shared state space on top of which planning and tool use become easier; (b) boundary with L5: at L4, generation is means in policy loop (CoT-VLA predicts visual states for downstream control); at L5, generation itself must encode causal world dynamics. Key Challenge. The defining challenge at L4 is grounded verification under intervention: the system must verify not only the final image but each intermediate decisionwhat to search, what tool to invoke, when 10 to stopagainst whether it is the right next action for the current state. The harder system pushes from workflowtoward agent-side, the more its trajectory depends on its own decision quality. Memory and retrieval (GEMS, Gen-Searcher) extend competence yet expose new failure modes: noisy evidence, brittle verifier signals, trajectories optimizing proxies rather than the goal. Tool-grounded settings (JarvisArt) demand correct executable edits with content preservation and local precision, not just plausible pixels. L4 systems fail not because they cannot render, but because they cannot yet reliably decide, verify, and correct inside an open-ended loop. Section 7 Dimensions III and IV surface this through fragile reasoning traces and silent drift in multi-turn editing. Table 1 Comparative taxonomy of visual intelligence. Our five-level framework organizes progress in visual generation as nested expansion of capability, drawing parallels to OpenAIs general AI roadmap. Each level subsumes its predecessors while introducing qualitatively new competence, distinct set of representative methods, and defining challenge that separates statistical plausibility from genuine visual intelligence. The One-Line Definition column captures the operational signature of each levelmost importantly, that L3 remains single forward pass over rich context, while L4 is the regime where multiple forward passes are stitched by an external control flow. Level Visual Paradigm One-Line Definition Core Characteristic Representative Methods Key Challenge AI Counterpart L1 Atomic Generation Conditional Generation L3 In-Context Generation L4 Agentic Generation World-Modeling Generation (future) One forward pass without explicit constraints; sample plausible image from text alone One forward pass with single explicit condition (structure / reference / source-image + instruction) One forward pass over rich context: multi-reference, multi-condition, accumulated history, or multi-output Multiple forward passes with control flow: plan, verify, invoke tools, refine Generation anchored by an internalized world model; discriminative tasks and domain-structured generation emerge as consequences Stochastic Plausibility (Distribution Matching) DDPM, DiT, LDM/SD, LlamaGen, VAR Uncontrolled variation; no spatial precision OpenAI L1 (Chatbots) Compositional Controllability ControlNet, IP-Adapter, GLIGEN, SD Attribute binding & spatial precision Transition toward Reasoning Contextual Coherence (Visual Logic & Memory) SEED-Data-Edit, ImgEdit, StoryMaker, Visual Persona Identity preservation under state change OpenAI L2 (Reasoners) Closed-loop Agency (Perception-Action-Feedback) GEMS, Gen-Searcher, JarvisArt, CoT-VLA Grounded verification & self-correction OpenAI L3 (Agents) Causal Simulation (Physics & Intervention) Genie 2, GameNGen, Oasis, UniSim, GAIACausal faithfulness; physical plausibility OpenAI L4 (Innovators)

sections.title: 2.5 Level 5: World-Modeling Generation (future)
sections.content: Definition. L5 is reached when model internalizes stable representations of dynamics, physics, and intervention effectsa world simulator, not just an appearance generator. Where L4 agents reason from learned correlations, L5 demands prediction of what would actually happen under specific action in specific physical context. The most fully realized L5 demonstrations today appear in interactive video and embodied settings; we treat them as case studies of L5 capability, not as methodological focus. Representative Methods. Neural game engines provide the most vivid demonstrations: Genie 2 (ParkerHolder et al., 2024) generates 3D environments learned from passive video, with persistent object behavior; GameNGen (Valevski et al., 2024) replaces DOOMs hand-coded engine with diffusion model rendering frames conditioned on player actions at interactive frame rates; Oasis (Quevedo et al., 2024) scales this to Minecraft. In embodied settings, UniSim (Yang et al., 2024c) is universal visual simulator for manipulation and navigation; GAIA-1 (Hu et al., 2023) generates controllable driving scenarios conditioned on vehicle actions. Image-side, L5 also encompasses world-knowledge grounding: rendering scenarios that respect gravity, fluid dynamics, material properties, or domain rulesprobed by benchmarks like PhyBench (Meng et al., 2024) and WISE (Niu et al., 2025). Key Challenge. The decisive challenge is causal faithfulness: whether the generated future state reflects the causal consequences of an intervention, not merely its statistical correlates. photorealistic rollout that ignores the commanded steering or misrepresents contact dynamics is worse than useless for downstream policy learningit corrupts the training signal. Current models learn correlations without internalizing physical laws and fail under unusual mechanisms or extreme maneuvers. Evaluation itself is fundamental barrier at L5: judging whether scene is physically correct and causally consistent requires benchmarks beyond perceptual qualityverifying how scene works, not what it looks like. We examine this gap through 11 fluid-dynamics counterfactuals (Section 7.3.1), action-conditioned driving (Section 7.3.2), robotic manipulation (Section 7.3.3), and trajectory synthesis (Section 7.3.4).

sections.title: 3 Model, Architecture, and Method
sections.content: This section answers three questions about how frontier visual generation system is built. Section 3.1 asks how generation is mathematically modeled, tracing the historical progression from GANs through diffusion, flow matching, autoregressive, and hybrid AR+diffusion paradigms as sequence of moves where each generation removes key bottleneck of the previous one. Section 3.2 asks what components make up system and how they cooperate, decomposing every modern model into an encoder/tokenizer, backbone, condition module, and multimodal fusion modulea decomposition that increasingly turns generation and editing into two data flows through the same architecture. Section 3.3 asks what can be inferred when the strongest systems disclose only behavior rather than architecture: it turns the convergence and bifurcation observed in open reports into future-facing discussion of closed-source agent loops, stronger upstream VLMs, and the system-level gaps that open models still need to close. The reader can treat this section as an entry point into the internals of todays frontier openand closed-source systems, anchored to the L1L5 capability levels of Section 2.

sections.title: 3.1 Foundational Generative Paradigms
sections.content: The history of generative modeling for images can be read as sequence of paradigms, each one defined by the limitation of the previous generation it sets out to remove. Generative adversarial networks (GANs) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) first demonstrated that neural network could turn random noise directly into perceptually convincing images, but their adversarial training was notoriously unstable, prone to mode collapse, and difficult to scale beyond curated single-domain datasets. Diffusion models (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021b) answered exactly that limitation: by replacing the adversarial game with iterative denoising under tractable likelihood objective, they made training stable and easy to scale to web-scale data; once paired with cross-attention conditioning as in LDM (Rombach et al., 2022), the same recipe also became amenable to text conditioningat the cost of hundreds of sequential network evaluations per sample. Flow matching and rectified flow (Liu et al., 2022b; Lipman et al., 2023) then attacked precisely that sampling bottleneck by learning straight-line transport between noise and data, which is simulation-free to train and integrable in only handful of steps. In parallel, the rise of large language models motivated an autoregressive reading of vision: LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024) and VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) discretize images into tokens and predict them next-token or next-scale, while Chameleon (Chameleon Team, 2024) and Emu3 (Wang et al., 2024c) push the same protocol to fully unified multimodal token streamtrading some per-pixel fidelity for single architecture that natively reasons across text and image. prominent frontier direction is now hybridization (Chen et al., 2025c; Cao et al., 2025): an autoregressive model decides what to generate at the level of plan tokens, while diffusion or flow model decides how to render the corresponding pixels, combining ARs instruction-following with diffusions per-pixel fidelity. Figure 5 summarizes the five paradigms side by side, allowing the reader to compare their core mechanisms before we examine each in detail. 3.1.1 GAN Overview. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) synthesize images by training generator and discriminator in minimax game; while no longer the frontier paradigm, they remain industrially relevant for one-step inference and fine-grained latent control. Mathematical Formulation. The original GAN objective is the minimax problem min max V (D, G) = Expdata[log D(x)] + Ezpz [log(1 D(G(z)))], (1) which reaches equilibrium when pG = pdata. In practice, the generator update is replaced by the non-saturating loss minG Ez[ log D(G(z))] to avoid vanishing gradients early in training, and the WGAN family (Arjovsky et al., 2017) swaps the JensenShannon divergence for the Earth-Mover distance under Lipschitz constraint to further stabilize the dynamics. 12 Figure 4 Timeline of representative image generation models by generative paradigm. Nodes are positioned by first public release month when available and grouped into GAN, diffusion / flow-matching, autoregressive, and hybrid categories. Hybrid nodes use both autoregressive and diffusion / flow colours to emphasize that recent frontier systems increasingly combine semantic planning with high-fidelity rendering. Figure 5 Five generative-modeling paradigms at glance. (1) GAN: generator maps noise to fake image while discriminator separates real from fake under an adversarial loss. (2) Diffusion Model (DM): forward chain x0 xT adds noise; learned reverse chain denoises along curved sampling trajectory. (3) Flow Matching (FM): noise is transported to data along straight-line ODE trajectory, simulation-free to train and integrable in few steps. (4) Autoregressive (AR): an image is discretized into tokens predicted next-token (raster) or next-scale (coarse-to-fine pyramid). (5) Hybrid (AR + Diffusion): an AR planner emits plan tokens that condition diffusion renderer producing the final image. 13 Representative Work. Subsequent research advanced GANs along four directions, each addressing distinct limitation of the original minimax recipe. Training stability is improved by Wasserstein and Lipschitzcontrolled objectives (WGAN-GP (Gulrajani et al., 2017), LSGAN (Mao et al., 2017), SN-GAN (Miyato et al., 2018)). Architectural scaling progresses from convolutional DCGAN (Radford et al., 2016) through ProGAN (Salimans et al., 2022) and BigGAN (Brock et al., 2019) to the style-based StyleGAN family (Karras et al., 2019), which introduced disentangled fine-grained control. Conditional control extends GANs to label-, text-, or image-driven generation (Conditional/InfoGAN (Chen et al., 2016), CycleGAN (Zhu et al., 2020), Pix2Pix (Isola et al., 2017)). Fast inference keeps GANs industrially relevant in the diffusion era through transformer-scale designs (GigaGAN (Kang et al., 2023)), modernized adversarial objectives such as R3GAN (Huang et al., 2025a), or by fusing adversarial losses with diffusion training to produce one-step samplers. Summary. GANs have receded from the prompt-following frontier but remain the recipe of choice when one-shot synthesis speed and fine-grained latent control matter more than the broader instruction fidelity that diffusion-based systems now lead.

sections.title: 3.1.2 Diffusion and Flow Matching
sections.content: Mathematical Formulation. Both diffusion models and flow matching belong to the broader family of continuous-time generative models. They share unifying principle: learning deterministic or stochastic transformation between simple tractable distribution p0 (e.g., Gaussian noise) and the complex data distribution p1. Diffusion models originally framed this as reversing gradual noising process. forward process corrupts data x1 into noise x0 over time, and the model learns denoising network ϵθ(xt, t) to reverse it: Ex1,ϵ,t (cid:2)ϵ ϵθ(xt, t)2(cid:3) . min θ (2) Crucially, as established by score-based SDEs (Song et al., 2021b), this reverse process can be described by Probability Flow ODE. This revealed that diffusion models are implicitly learning vector field that defines the trajectory from noise to data. Flow Matching (FM) and Rectified Flow directly generalize this ODE perspective. Instead of relying on predefined forward noising process, FM constructs vector field that transports samples along explicit, often straighter, probability paths. Given x0 p0 and x1 p1, simple linear interpolation is defined as: xt = (1 t) x0 + x1, U[0, 1]. (3) The target velocity along this path is straightforwardly vtarget(xt, t) = x1 x0. The model directly learns this velocity field vθ(xt, t) via simulation-free regression objective: LFM(θ) = Ex0,x1,t h(cid:13) (cid:13)vθ(xt, t) (x1 x0)(cid:13) (cid:13) 2i . (4) During inference, both paradigms generate data by solving their respective ordinary differential equations starting from x0 p0. Representative Work. DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) re-established likelihood-based image generation by learning denoiser that inverts fixed forward noising process, but its strong sample quality came with major practical drawback: high-fidelity synthesis required hundreds to thousands of reverse steps. DDIM (Song et al., 2021a) showed that this sampling cost is separable from the training objective. By constructing non-Markovian deterministic reverse trajectory, it retained DDPM training while reducing inference cost by an order of magnitude, making explicit that sampler design is an independent axis of innovation. Score-based SDE models (Song et al., 2021b) pushed this insight further by recasting diffusion as continuoustime stochastic process with an equivalent probability-flow ODE. This continuous-time view unified discrete diffusion, ODE solvers, and likelihood computation under single framework, turning sampling into numerical integration problem with an explicit accuracycompute trade-off. Rectified Flow (Liu et al., 2022b) 14 and Flow Matching (Lipman et al., 2023) then shifted the focus from solving increasingly curved reverse trajectories to learning straighter transport paths directly. In particular, Flow Matching removed the need for expensive ODE simulation during training and made ODE-based generators practical at modern scale. Modern systems increasingly pair these objectives with transformer backbones. SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) shows that rectified-flow objectives compose naturally with the transformer scaling recipe, yielding strong prompt fidelity and typography when combined with MM-DiT (the modality-aware DiT variant; we discuss its architecture in Section 3.2.2). Diff2Flow (Schusterbauer et al., 2025) further narrows the gap between diffusion and flow by converting pretrained diffusion checkpoints into flow-matching generators through schedule alignment. Taken together, the field has evolved from discrete denoising chains to broader family of continuous transport models whose main practical question is no longer whether diffusion or flow is better in principle, but how to co-design objective, sampler, and backbone for low-NFE high-fidelity generation.

sections.title: 3.1.3 Autoregressive Models
sections.content: Mathematical Formulation. Autoregressive (AR) generative models produce sequences by predicting each token conditioned on all previously generated tokens. Given sequence x1:T = (x1, . . . , xT ), an AR model factorizes the joint distribution as pθ(x1:T ) = t=1 pθ(xt x<t), (5) where x<t = (x1, . . . , xt1) and θ denotes model parameters. In practice, this factorization is commonly implemented using decoder-only Transformer trained to maximize the log-likelihood of observed sequences. During inference, generation proceeds sequentially by sampling from pθ(xt x<t) until termination condition, such as an end-of-sequence token, is reached. Representative Work. Recent progress in autoregressive visual generation is better illustrated by image and multimodal systems than by text-only language models. LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024) shows that vanilla decoder-only transformer trained with next-token prediction on discrete image tokens can match diffusion baselines when paired with an effective tokenizer, sufficient scale, and strong data. VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) makes the AR recipe more efficient by replacing raster-scan prediction with next-scale prediction in coarse-to-fine hierarchy, reducing the effective sequence length and becoming the first AR model to surpass DiT in both FID and generation speed. second wave extends the same next-token objective from pure image synthesis to unified multimodal modeling. Chameleon (Chameleon Team, 2024) and Emu3 (Wang et al., 2024c) treat images and text as single mixed-modal token stream, showing that early-fusion autoregression can support both understanding and generation without dedicated diffusion decoder. Janus-Pro (Chen et al., 2025k), BAGEL (Deng et al., 2025), and OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025) scale this idea further through better training data, larger unified token streams, and more efficient sequence backbones. The resulting picture is that AR models are most compelling when the goal is not only image synthesis, but also sharing common token protocol with language and multimodal reasoning. We discuss the architectural mechanisms that enable this multimodal unificationtoken-stream design, encoder decoupling, and fusion strategiesin Section 3.2.4. 3.1.4 Hybrid AR + Diffusion/Flow Models Formulation. Hybrid systems explicitly separate semantic planning from visual rendering. common factorization introduces an intermediate plan z: pθ(x c) = pϕ(z c) pψ(x z, c) dz, (6) where an autoregressive model predicts the plan zfor example, mixed-modal token sequence, semantic layout, or continuous latent prefixand diffusion or flow model renders high-fidelity visual details conditioned on that plan. The motivation is pragmatic: AR models naturally support causal reasoning, long-context instruction following, and tight integration with language, while diffusion/flow models remain stronger at spatially coherent, high-fidelity synthesis. 15 Figure 6 Paradigm landscape of representative visual generation architectures. Methods are positioned by their generative mechanism: diffusion/flow matching (left), autoregressive (right), and adversarial (bottom). The central overlap hosts hybrid architectures that combine diffusion and autoregressive components. denotes unified models that jointly support visual understanding and generation. Representative Work. Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2024a) is canonical early-fusion design: it interleaves discrete text tokens and continuous image latents within one transformer, using cross-entropy for text and diffusion loss for image patches. MonoFormer (Zhao et al., 2024a) pushes the shared-backbone idea further by using pretrained LLM-style transformer for both AR text generation and latent diffusion image generation, switching only the attention mask and prediction head: causal attention with an AR head for text, and bidirectional attention with diffusion head for noised VAE latents. Show-o (Xie et al., 2024) reaches similar goal through attention-mask switching, unifying AR text generation and discrete diffusion image generation in one backbone; Show-o2 (Xie et al., 2025) updates the image branch to flow matching in 3D causal VAE space. JanusFlow (Ma et al., 2024e) combines an AR LLM with rectified flow through decoupled visual encoders and representation alignment, while BLIP3o-NEXT (Chen et al., 2025c) adopts an explicit cascade in which an AR stage plans semantics and diffusion stage renders pixels from AR hidden states. MAR (Li et al., 2024b) and NextStep-1 (Team et al., 2025b) make the coupling even tighter by retaining an AR backbone but replacing the categorical token head with token-wise diffusion over continuous latents. Across these methods, the shared conclusion is not that AR should replace diffusion, or vice versa, but that the two families solve different subproblems. Hybridization is therefore central design pattern, not niche exceptionand the same AR plans, diffusion renders division reappears at the system level, prefiguring the L4 agentic-generation regime of Section 2 and the closed-source agent loop discussed in Section 3.3.

sections.title: 3.2 Architecture Components
sections.content: Beyond cataloging components, this subsection delivers single message: generation and editing have collapsed into the same architecture. As recently as 2024, image editing was almost always separate paper with its own backbone fork; within roughly year, the clear majority of frontier openand closed-source systems present T2I and editing as two data flows through one model, where the task is determined entirely by what enters the encoder and condition module rather than by different network (Figure 7). Three camps achieve this unification differently. The DiT camp (Seedream et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025a; Cai et al., 2025; Joy Future Academy, JD, 2026; Team et al., 2025a; Mao et al., 2026) injects reference images as VAE-latent tokens 16 Table 2 Component-level morphology of representative visual generation architectures. marks defining design choice; indicates the component is not used. The table is intentionally schematic: checkmark denotes the primary component of each method, not the only possible configuration. AR = Autoregressive; Rect. Flow = Rectified Flow. This table presents methods through modeling-paradigm lens (objective + backbone + representation + conditioning); the complementary Table 3 re-examines partly overlapping set of methods through multimodal-fusion lens, so paper overlap reflects two views of the same systems rather than redundancy. Method Objective Backbone Representation Conditioning - / C r sfo Tr el Pix e la / t la Q e Dis a - r d. E / d m t alu k e nifi Diff Diffusion Diffusion LDM (Rombach et al., 2022) DiT (Peebles and Xie, 2023) SD3 / MM-DiT (Esser et al., 2024) Rect. Flow Diffusion RAE-DiT (Zheng et al., 2025a) Diffusion SVG (Shi et al., 2025a) Diffusion JiT (Li and He, 2025) Adversarial R3GAN (Huang et al., 2025a) AR LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024) AR VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) AR Janus-Pro (Chen et al., 2025k) AR Bridge (Wang et al., 2025d) AR OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025) Hybrid Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2024a) Monoformer (Zhao et al., 2024a) Hybrid BLIP3o-NEXT (Chen et al., 2025c) Hybrid Figure 7 The unified architecture for visual generation and editing. modern visual generation / editing pipeline contains an encoder / tokenizer, condition routing, generative backbone, and an output decoder arranged left-toright. (1) Encoder / Tokenizer accepts heterogeneous inputs (text prompt, optional reference image, optional source image, optional structural conditions such as depth / edge / layout) via VAE, VQ, SigLIP, or CLIP. (2) Condition Module routes the encoded condition into the backbone through AdaLN modulation, cross-attention, or in-context concatenation. (3) Backbone (DiT, MM-DiT, or AR-transformer) does the heavy compute. (4) Decoder (VAE / VQ / pixel) produces the final image. The decoder is shown as the output endpoint, but its design is tied to the representation choice; the four axes analyzed below are representation, backbone, condition routing, and multimodal fusion. The same pipeline supports both T2I (text generated image, blue path) and editing (text + source image edited image, red path)only the inputs entering the encoder and condition module differ. 17 disambiguated by 3D-RoPE and different timestep, then trusts MM-DiT to absorb both flows. The AR camp (Cao et al., 2025; Team et al., 2026a) treats every image as token sequence so that T2I and editing become different contexts to the same next-token predictor. The hybrid camp (Chen et al., 2025c) factorizes into an MLLM planner and DiT visualizer that share an attention backbone. Each camp resolves different open trade-off: DiT systems inherit mature distillation toolchain that dominates the few-step inference frontier, while discrete-AR systems argue that their finite-MDP structure makes reinforcement learning more natural. The active question in this corner of the field is no longer whether to unify, but which mechanism delivers the better Pareto frontier. Community Message: Edit Unification in One Year year ago Seedream 3.0 (Gao et al., 2025) (early 2025) treated image editing as separate paper from text-to-image; today, by Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026) and LongCat-Next (Team et al., 2026a) (spring 2026), 60% of recent frontier reports ship fully unified architecturea where T2I, I2I, and instruction-based editing share the same backbone. The debate has shifted from whether to unify to which mechanism unifies best: DiT-style triplet (VAE + RoPE + timestep injection of the reference) versus an ARstyle image-as-token, task decided by context framing, with hybrid bets like Wan-Image splitting the difference. The largest open trade-off is distillation-friendliness vs. RL-friendliness: AR-style unification is easier to RL but harder to compress to few steps; DiT-style unification is the reverse. This single trade-off shapes which design will dominate the 2026 cohort, and is currently the most consequential unsolved question in image-generation architecture. aThe cohort consists of ten public 20252026 image-generation tech reports we read side by side: Seedream 3.0 (Gao et al., 2025), Seedream 4.0 (Seedream et al., 2025), HunyuanImage 3.0 (Cao et al., 2025), Qwen-Image (Wu et al., 2025a), Z-Image (Cai et al., 2025), LongCat-Image (Team et al., 2025a), FireRed-Image-Edit-1.0 (Team et al., 2026b), JoyAI-Image (Joy Future Academy, JD, 2026), LongCat-Next (Team et al., 2026a), and Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026). The four axes below are largely orthogonal  (Table 2)  : the same backbone can sit on top of VAE latents or discrete tokens, and the same condition module serves both diffusion and autoregressive backbones, which is what most new methods exploit by innovating on one or two axes while inheriting established designs on the others. The output decoder is still part of the pipeline, but because its VAE / VQ / pixel choice is tied to representation, we discuss it under encoder/tokenizer rather than as standalone axis. 1. Encoder / Tokenizer (3.2.1): the entry point of the pipeline. It maps raw pixels into compact representation spacecontinuous latents or discrete tokenswhere generation becomes computationally tractable. 2. Backbone network (3.2.2): the central compute unit that operates in the encoded space, parameterizing the denoising step (diffusion), velocity prediction (flow matching), next-token generation (autoregressive), or staged combination of these primitives (hybrid models). 3. Condition module (3.2.3): translates external control signals (text prompts, spatial maps, reference images) into features that steer the backbones output toward user intent. 4. Multimodal fusion module (3.2.4): determines where and how information from different modalities interacts within the backbone, from simple cross-attention injection to fully unified token streams. 3.2.1 Encoder, Tokenizer, and Representation Space Generating images directly in pixel space is computationally prohibitive: 512 512 RGB image has 786,432 dimensions. The encoder (or tokenizer) is the entry point of the generation pipelineit maps raw pixels into compact representation space where the backbone can operate efficiently. The encoder thus controls fundamental trade-off: aggressive compression speeds up generation but may lose fine-grained visual details; conservative compression preserves fidelity but limits scalability. The choice of representation is therefore not merely preprocessing detail: it determines which objectives are natural (reconstruction, next-token prediction, or noise/velocity prediction), how efficiently the backbone scales, and whether understanding and generation can share common token protocol. 18 Continuous Latents: VAE and Beyond. The dominant approach for diffusion-based models is the variational autoencoder (VAE). LDM (Rombach et al., 2022) trains KL-regularized autoencoder that compresses images by 8 spatially, achieving near-optimal balance between complexity reduction and perceptual fidelity. Generation then occurs entirely in this latent space, reducing the backbones computational burden by 64. recent trend moves beyond trainable encoders toward representation-first designs that leverage frozen pretrained models. RAE (Zheng et al., 2025a) replaces the VAE encoder with frozen discriminative features (e.g., DINOv2, SigLIP) and trains only lightweight decoder, finding that DiT models converge substantially faster on these semantically rich latents. SVG (Shi et al., 2025a) pushes this further by removing the VAE entirely, performing diffusion directly on frozen DINOv2 features with residual branch for pixel recovery. REPA (Yu et al., 2024) takes complementary approach: rather than replacing the encoder, it adds an auxiliary alignment loss during training that pulls noisy intermediate denoiser states toward clean-image representations from pretrained encoder, improving training efficiency and generation quality without any architectural change. Discrete Tokens: VQ for Autoregressive and Unified Models. Autoregressive generation requires discrete vocabulary. Vector-quantized (VQ) tokenizers map image patches to codebook entries, enabling standard next-token prediction. The codebook quality directly limits generation fidelity: VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) requires multi-scale VQ-VAE producing hierarchical token maps at multiple resolutions so the backbone can perform its next-scale prediction. An active frontier is multimodal tokenizationdesigning token spaces that serve both vision and language. TokenFlow (Qu et al., 2024) uses dual-codebook design to decouple semantic and pixel-level information while preserving alignment between them. Vision as Dialect (Han et al., 2025) introduces TA-Tok, whose codebook is projected from an LLM vocabulary so that image and text tokens share discrete semantic space. Bridge (Wang et al., 2025d) proposes semantic-to-pixel discrete representation: short semantic-token prefix followed by longer pixel-token suffix, guiding the model to plan semantics before filling details. Janus-Pro (Chen et al., 2025k) resolves the same invariance-versus-variance tension through separate visual encodersSigLIP for understanding and VQ tokens for generationbehind shared AR backbone. UAE (Yan et al., 2025) pushes the bottleneck further by treating text as the shared interface between image understanding and image generation. Industrial-scale autoregressive systems push tokenization further still with residual VQ (RVQ), which interleaves multiple codebook lookups per patch to recover fidelity that single codebook would lose: LongCat-Nexts dNaViT tokenizer (Team et al., 2026a) stacks eight residual codebooks of size 16,384 at 28 spatial compression and dispenses with VAE entirely, supplying both image and (separately quantized) audio tokens directly to the AR backbone. Can We Skip the Tokenizer Entirely? JiT (Li and He, 2025) challenges the encoder paradigm by feeding raw pixel patches directly to transformer. While this sacrifices latent-space efficiency, it eliminates reconstruction artifacts entirelyan appealing property when pixel-level fidelity matters (e.g., medical imaging or document generation). Representation Trend. Viewed together, recent representation designs trace clear progression: VAE latents minimize compute for diffusion, discrete codebooks enable AR scaling and multimodal token sharing, SSL-aligned latents inject stronger semantics into diffusion backbones, and tokenizer-free pixel representations eliminate reconstruction artifacts altogether. Representation choice has therefore become co-evolving part of the model design rather than fixed front-end. In practice, the VAE itself remains unsettled at the frontier: FLUX-VAE (Labs et al., 2025), the Wan-2.1 VAE adopted by Qwen-Image (Wu et al., 2025a) (single-encoder dual-decoder, decoder fine-tuned for text-rich domains), self-trained 4-channel RGBA designs (Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026), with 16 compression and 48 latent channels), and VAE-free RVQ tokenizers (LongCat-Next) all coexist across 2026 reports, indicating that compression ratio, latent dimension, and reconstruction-fidelity trade-offs still carry unresolved choices.

sections.title: 3.2.2 Backbone Networks
sections.content: Given the encoded representationwhether continuous latents from VAE or discrete tokens from VQ codebookthe backbone is the central compute unit that performs the actual generation: denoising (diffusion), velocity prediction (flow matching), or next-token generation (autoregressive). The choice of backbone determines the models inductive bias (local vs. global reasoning), scaling behavior (how quality improves with compute), and inference speed. The field has evolved through three generations, each unlocking new capabilities. From U-Net to Transformer: The Scaling Transition. Early diffusion models adopted the convolutional U-Net architecture, whose hierarchical feature pyramids naturally match multi-resolution image statistics. LDM (Stable Diffusion) (Rombach et al., 2022) made this practical at scale by performing diffusion in compressed latent space rather than pixel space, with text conditioning injected via cross-attention layers. However, U-Nets lack the predictable computequality scaling later observed in transformer backbones. DiT (Peebles and Xie, 2023) addressed this by replacing the U-Net entirely with plain vision transformer operating on patchified latents. The key finding was monotonic FID improvement with increasing FLOPs, establishing that transformer-based diffusion follows predictable scaling laws analogous to those in language modeling. Conditioning is provided through adaptive layer normalization (AdaLN), which regresses scale and shift parameters from timestep-class embeddinglighter than cross-attention and sufficient for classconditional generation. SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) extended this with the MM-DiT (Modality-aware DiT) architecture, which maintains separate weight streams for text and image tokens while enabling bidirectional information exchange via joint attention. This dual-stream design substantially improved text comprehension and typography fidelity, establishing the architectural template adopted by subsequent industrial systems such as the FLUX family (Labs et al., 2025). Two recent variants probe the limits of the dual-stream choice. Z-Image (Cai et al., 2025) is notable counter-move: its S3-DiT (Single-Stream DiT) collapses MM-DiTs dual streams back into single attention path, arguing that stronger upstream VLM and tighter data curation make modalityspecific weights redundantan explicit test of whether the MM-DiT gain is structural or compensatory. complementary backbone-internal hybrid keeps MM-DiT layers in the lower stack and switches to singlestream DiT in the upper stack once cross-modal binding has been established (FLUX.1-dev style, adopted by LongCat-Image (Team et al., 2025a) with 10:20 ratio of MM-DiT to single-stream blocks). Autoregressive Backbones: Unifying Language and Vision. parallel line of work reframes image generation as sequence prediction problem, enabling direct reuse of language model architectures. LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024) demonstrated that standard decoder-only transformer with next-token prediction on discrete image tokens can match diffusion baselines given sufficient scale, validating visual scaling laws for the autoregressive paradigm. VAR (Tian et al., 2024a) introduced more efficient generation order: instead of raster-scan token prediction, it performs next-scale prediction in coarse-to-fine hierarchy, reducing effective sequence length from O(n2) to O(n log n)the canonical AR backbone alternative to raster-scan, with the FID and speed comparison against DiT discussed earlier in Section 3.1. Emerging Architectures. Beyond transformers, several alternative backbones have emerged. OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025) builds on the Mamba-2 state-space model, reporting up to 119.2 speedup and 63% GPU-memory reduction relative to Transformer-based counterparts on long-sequence generationparticularly relevant for high-resolution generation where sequence lengths explode, regime that becomes the binding constraint at L3 in-context generation in Section 2. At the other extreme, JiT (Li and He, 2025) demonstrates that pixel-space transformers operating without any tokenizer can achieve competitive quality when switching from noise prediction to direct x-prediction, trading latent-space efficiency for elimination of reconstruction artifacts. Hybrid model families are also gaining traction, but their main novelty lies in how they factorize planning and rendering rather than in wholly new backbone primitive; we therefore discuss them at the paradigm level in Section 3.1.4. Sparse-Expert Backbones. second axis along which 2026 frontier systems split is parameter sparsity. HunyuanImage 3.0 (Cao et al., 2025) routes through 64-expert, top-8 mixture-of-experts with 80B total / 13B 20 active parameters, and LongCat-Next (Team et al., 2026a) applies the same sparse-expert recipe to discreteAR backbone with 68.5B total / A3B active parameters. The bet is capacity without proportional inference cost; the cost is router stability and heavier training pipeline. Sparse-expert vision backbones remain rare relative to dense designsQwen-Image (Wu et al., 2025a), Z-Image (Cai et al., 2025), Seedream (Seedream et al., 2025), and LongCat-Image (Team et al., 2025a) all stay denseindicating that the MoE-versus-dense trade-off is not yet settled at the frontier, and that the MoE bet is currently concentrated in systems that already commit to AR-style backbones where sparse routing composes naturally with token-level prediction.

sections.title: 3.2.3 Condition Module
sections.content: A generative model without conditioning produces random samples from the learned distribution. The condition module is what makes generation controllable: it translates external signalstext prompts, spatial or visual conditions, reference images, or class labelsinto features that steer the backbones output toward user intent. To achieve this systematically without disrupting the underlying modeling objective, modern architectures decouple this process into two fundamental stages: Condition Feature Extraction (how to encode the external signal) and Feature Injection (how to fuse it into the backbone). Figure 8 Three condition injection routes in modern transformer-based generators. (a) AdaLN modulation: condition predicts scale and shift parameters of LayerNorm via small MLP (used in DiT). (b) Cross-attention: condition flows into the K/V of an additional cross-attention layer (used in Stable Diffusions U-Net, IP-Adapter). (c) In-context concatenation: condition tokens are prepended to image tokens in the same sequence and processed by self-attention (used in MM-DiT, OmniGen, Bridge). Condition Feature Extraction. The first step is transforming raw conditional inputs into latent representation compatible with the generative backbone. Depending on the complexity and modality of the condition, three dominant extraction paradigms have emerged: Adapter on Copied Backbone: For spatially dense conditions (e.g., depth maps, edge maps, or poses), preserving exact spatial alignment is critical. Methods like ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023a) and SCEdit (Jiang et al., 2024b) freeze the pretrained backbone and attach trainable, initialized-to-zero copy to extract spatial features. ControlNet++ (Li et al., 2024a) further refines this by incorporating discriminative reward model to enforce cycle consistency between input conditions and generated outputs, ensuring higher fidelity in spatial extraction. CoLoGen (Song et al., 2026) frames unified conditional generation as representation-conflict problem, separating concept-level semantics from localization-level spatial control instead of forcing all conditions through single shared adapter. Frozen Vision Foundation Models (VFMs): When the condition is reference image requiring semantic or identity preservation, models typically leverage frozen pretrained VFMs (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP, or VAEs). Lumina-mGPT (Liu et al., 2024a) relies on VQ-VAE-style image tokenizer to convert images into discrete tokens, enabling decoder-only mGPT to autoregressively model interleaved textimage sequences. Explanatory Instructions (Shen et al., 2024) builds on token-based AR VLM with an image tokenizer/de-tokenizer, using linguistic transformations between tokenized input-output images to unify diverse vision tasks. IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023) extracts global CLIP image embeddings, while recent unified frameworks (OmniGen (Wu et al., 2025b), Qwen-Image (Wu et al., 2025a), Z-Image (Cai et al., 2025)) extract VAE latents and SigLIP patch features in parallel to represent visual prompts. The two streams are complementary: VLM-derived semantic features carry instruction intent (what to draw), while VAE-derived pixel features preserve fine-grained appearance (how it should look)a division of labor that the Qwen-Image report identifies as the source of its TI2I gains. Adapter on Vision-Language Models (VLMs): As editing tasks become more complex, simple visual or textual extraction is insufficient. Recent methods employ powerful VLMs to perform reasoning before conditioning. Systems like Step-1X-Edit (Liu et al., 2025h), Query-Kontext (Song et al., 2025b) and Qwen-Image-Edit (Wu et al., 2025a) use VLMs to parse complex instructions and extract reasoningaware conditional features. Similarly, Bridge (Wang et al., 2025d) introduces semantic planning tokens generated prior to rendering, guiding the backbone with high-level semantic intent. Feature Injection Mechanisms. Once the conditional features are extracted, they must be routed into the backbone. The choice of injection mechanism dictates the trade-off between expressiveness, computational cost, and modularity: Feature-wise Addition: The most direct approach for spatially aligned features is to add the extracted condition directly to the intermediate feature maps. ControlNet and SCEdit utilize this mechanism, bypassing complex attention calculations while ensuring strict spatial constraint propagation. Cross-Attention: Popularized by LDM (Rombach et al., 2022), this projects conditioning embeddings into key-value pairs that attend to the backbones queries. While IP-Adapter and InstantID (Wang et al., 2024a) use separate cross-attention layers, SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) employs dual-stream joint attention, allowing the image and text branches to attend to each other bidirectionally, which substantially improves typography and prompt adherence. In-Context Self-Attention: In unified autoregressive and DiT-based models, conditioning is treated as token-level protocol. Extracted condition tokens are prepended or interleaved with generation tokens and processed jointly via shared self-attention. This is the core mechanism in OmniGen, Qwen-Image, and Z-Image. OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025) enhances this with decoupled modality vocabularies and task-specific LoRA adapters. MMaDA (Yang et al., 2025a) shows that the same token-level protocol can also be realized in unified diffusion backbone, where modality-agnostic tokens are denoised jointly rather than decoded causally. Reference-Image Injection in Unified Models. When the same model serves both T2I and editing, the source image must be routed into the backbone in way that does not collide with the noised target. Five mechanisms have emerged across recent frontier reports, and they are stackable rather than mutually exclusive. (i) VAE-latent token concat: source latents are concatenated with noised target latents along the sequence dimension (Seedream 4.0 (Seedream et al., 2025), Qwen-Image, JoyAI-Image (Joy Future Academy, JD, 2026), FireRed (Team et al., 2026b)). (ii) VLM/MLLM dual-stream: the source additionally passes through VLM (Qwen2.5-VL, SigLIP 2) whose semantic features supplement the text condition (Qwen-Image, Z-Image-Edit (Cai et al., 2025)). (iii) 3D-RoPE temporal-axis separation: reference and target tokens share spatial (x, y) RoPE coordinates but differ on temporal axis, letting attention disambiguate them (Z-Image-Edit, FireRed, Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026)). (iv) Different timestep conditioning: the clean reference uses t=0 while the noisy target uses normal timestep, exposing the cleanliness gap to the denoiser. (v) Context-as-prefix: in pure-AR systems (HunyuanImage 3.0 (Cao et al., 2025), LongCat-Next (Team et al., 2026a)), the source-image tokens simply precede target tokens as context, with no special routing. The mechanisms are routinely combined: FireRed pairs (i) with (iii), and Wan-Image stacks (i), (iii), and (iv). Global Modulation and Inference-Time Mechanisms. Beyond spatial and token-level injection, models rely on specialized mechanisms to handle global context and inference-time signal amplification: 22 Table 3 Representative unified understanding-and-generation architectures. Fusion strategy indicates where modalities first interact; visual repr. denotes the generation-side representation; gen. mechanism specifies how images are synthesized. Alignment-only methods are discussed separately in Section 4.2. This table is the multimodal-fusion-lens companion to the modeling-paradigm-lens Table 2; the two views overlap on shared papers by design. Method Year Fusion Strategy Visual Repr. Gen. Mechanism Key Innovation Chameleon (Chameleon Team, 2024) Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2024a) Show-o (Xie et al., 2024) Emu3 (Wang et al., 2024c) JanusFlow (Ma et al., 2024e) Bridge (Wang et al., 2025d) Janus-Pro (Chen et al., 2025k) BAGEL (Deng et al., 2025) Show-o2 (Xie et al., 2025) BLIP3o-NEXT (Chen et al., 2025c) OmniMamba (Zou et al., 2025) MMaDA (Yang et al., 2025a) UAE (Yan et al., 2025) HunyuanImage 3.0 (Cao et al., 2025) Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026) JoyAI-Image (Joy Future Academy, JD, 2026) 2026 Decoupled MLLM brain + MMDiT VLM hidden + VAE 2024 Unified token stream 2024 Unified token stream 2024 Unified token stream 2024 Unified token stream 2024 Decoupled encoder 2025 Separated-stream early fusion 2025 Decoupled encoder 2025 Unified token stream 2025 Hybrid heads 2025 ARDiff cascade 2025 Unified token stream 2025 Unified diffusion 2025 Encoder-decoder 2026 Unified token stream 2026 MLLM planner + DiT visualizer AR next-token Discrete VQ AR + Diffusion Continuous latent AR + Discrete diff. Discrete VQ AR next-token Discrete VQ AR + Rect. Flow Continuous latent Discrete semantic+pixel AR next-token VQ (gen) + SigLIP (und) AR next-token Interleaved multimodal 3D causal VAE Discretecont. Discrete VQ Modality-agnostic Text as bottleneck Continuous latent (32-ch) AR + Flow head VAE (4-ch RGBA) Early-fusion mixed-modal training recipe Hybrid loss: CE (text) + diffusion (image) Attention-mask switching per modality Pure next-token prediction for mixed-modal data LLM backbone harmonized with rectified flow Mixture-of-Transformers with semantic-to-pixel planning Separate visual pathways with shared AR backbone Trillion-token scale for unified reasoning and generation Flow-matching image head in shared causal backbone AR semantic planning followed by diffusion rendering Linear-time state-space backbone for long contexts Diffusion-native multimodal unification Text bottleneck linking understanding and generation Industrial-scale Transfusion: 80B/13B-active MoE ARFlow Bidirectional attention extends across an image series AR-conditioned diffusion 8B MLLM conditions 16B MMDiT via hidden states AR decoder-only AR + Flow matching ARDiffusion AR (SSM backbone) Diffusion (all mod.) AR enc.dec. Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN): Introduced in DiT (Peebles and Xie, 2023), AdaLN handles global conditions such as timesteps, class labels, and pooled text embeddings. It regresses scale and shift parameters from the condition to modulate feature statistics, providing lightweight alternative to attention-based injection. SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) combines AdaLN for timestep and class conditioning with attention-based injection for text, achieving both efficiency and expressiveness. Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG): CFG (Ho and Salimans, 2022) is an inference-time strategy rather than an architectural module. By jointly training conditional and unconditional branches, the model linearly extrapolates score estimates during sampling to amplify the conditioning signal. Increasing the guidance scale improves prompt adherence at the cost of reduced diversity. CFG remains the primary tool for controlling the fidelity-diversity trade-off across virtually all modern generation pipelines. Inference-Time Prompt Rewriting. Beyond architectural injection mechanisms, frontier systems increasingly augment the text condition itself before it ever reaches the encoder. The Prompt Engineering (PE) moduletypically small fine-tuned VLM such as Qwen3-VL-2B in Wan-Image, Seed1.5-VL in Seedream 3.0/4.0 (Gao et al., 2025; Seedream et al., 2025), or Gemini-class routerrewrites users short query into long detailed prompt aligned with the training distribution, performs task routing (T2I vs. editing vs. multi-reference), and optionally injects chain-of-thought reasoning trace; Wan-Images dual-mode design, for instance, uses non-CoT 2B model for short prompts and CoT 30B-A3B model for complex requests. PE is therefore not an architectural component of the generation backbone; it is an inference-time conditioning preprocessor that ensures the gen model receives prompts of the form it was trained on. This explains why high-quality short-prompt performance often correlates more with PE module quality than with backbone capacitya non-trivial industrial observation. 3.2.4 Multimodal Fusion Module As visual generation systems increasingly integrate language understanding and image synthesis within single model, critical architectural question emerges: at what stage should information from different modalities begin to interact? The fusion module answers this question. Following the taxonomy introduced by Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2024a), we distinguish between late fusion (modalities processed independently until final combination stage) and early fusion (modalities interacting within the backbones internal layers). The trend is decisively toward early fusion, but the degree of parameter sharing varies widelyfrom fully shared transformers to architectures that maintain separated processing streams while still enabling early-stage cross-modal interaction. Table 3 summarizes representative unified architectures along this fusion axis. We intentionally keep alignmentonly methods out of this summary: once the question shifts from where modalities meet to how unified model is post-trained to balance understanding and generation, the emphasis moves from architecture to alignment, which is discussed in Section 4.2. 23 Late Fusion: Modular but Limited. The simplest approach treats text as an external condition injected into vision-only backbone. LDM (Rombach et al., 2022) exemplifies this: frozen CLIP text embeddings are projected into cross-attention layers, creating one-directional flow from text to image. This design is modularthe text encoder and image generator can be developed independentlybut the lack of bidirectional interaction limits the models ability to handle complex prompts that require joint text-image reasoning. MetaQueries (Pan et al., 2025) is more modern bridge of the same flavor: it preserves strong multimodal understanding model and transfers its latent state to diffusion decoder through learnable queries, improving generation without yet making the backbone truly unified. Early Fusion with Separated Streams. growing family of models achieves early fusion while maintaining modality-specific processing pathways. SD3 (Esser et al., 2024) exemplifies this via its MM-DiT architecture: text and image tokens pass through modality-specific projection weights but share self-attention within each transformer block, enabling bidirectional cross-modal interaction at every layer. This dual-stream design substantially improves text comprehension and typography fidelity. Recent unified models extend this principle through specialized mechanisms. Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT), as used in Bridge (Wang et al., 2025d), assigns modality-specific expert parameters within shared backbone, enabling early interaction through shared attention while preserving per-modality specialization in the feedforward layers.
