In parallel, industrial labs have built their own holistic benchmarks to address three gaps left by the public ones: lag behind frontier capabilities, English-only coverage, and absence of productivity scenarios. HunyuanImage 3.0s SSAE (Cao et al., 2025) decomposes 500 prompts into 3,500 manually verified key points across 12 fine-grained semantic fields and locks the key-point set across all model comparisons, ensuring that cross-model differences reflect generation quality rather than evaluator drift. Seedream 4.0s MagicBench 4.0 (Seedream et al., 2025) explicitly bundles three task familiesT2I (325 prompts), singleimage editing (300 prompts), and multi-image editing (100 prompts)with bilingual coverage, while its companion DreamEval introduces tiered difficulty (Easy / Medium / Hard) over 1,600 prompts and 128 sub-tasks, scored through fine-grained VQA so that interpretability and headline numbers come from the same protocol. Seedream 3.0s earlier Bench-377 (Gao et al., 2025) contributes the productivity dimension explicitly, partitioning its 377 prompts across cinematic, arts, entertainment, aesthetic-design, and practicaldesign (Prac-Design) scenariosthe last of which targets icon arrangements in slides and illustration design in handwritten newspapers, content typically absent from research-oriented benchmarks. Although these suites are internally curated and not always publicly released, their methodological design choicesbilingual prompts, multi-task bundling, productivity scenarios, fixed key-point setsare increasingly visible in the public benchmarks discussed above. Community Message: Proxy Capabilities Are the Default Training-Progress Indicators Direct measurement of overall gen/edit quality is increasingly unreliable: FID and CLIP-score have effectively saturated at the frontier, and pairwise human evaluation does not scale to weekly trainingprogress checks. The community has therefore implicitly converged on set of proxy capabilities that are easy to measure, hard to game, and correlate well with overall capability lift: text rendering (English and Chinese)a, long-form text rendering, and structured-content rendering (flowcharts, chemical formulas, electrical schematics, mathematical layouts). The signal is concrete: JoyAI-Images (Joy Future Academy, JD, 2026) LongText-Bench EN/ZH score of 0.963 and LongCat-Images (Team et al., 2025a) 90.7 on ChineseWord versus Seedream 4.0s (Seedream et al., 2025) 58.5 (a 30-plus-point gap in single year) track real underlying-capability shifts that bulk perceptual metrics miss. The takeaway: when designing internal evaluation harnesses for frontier image-generation training run, instrument these proxies firstthey catch regressions and improvements weeks before bulk preference studies do. aThe Nano Banana team explicitly describes text rendering as training-progress indicator in their public interview: https://youtu.be/H6ZXujE1qBA?si=SdeAnvCwRTTY90oq.

sections.title: 5.2.9 Domain-Specific Benchmarks
sections.content: Beyond general-purpose evaluation, several benchmarks target specialized application domains where visual generation serves as component in larger pipeline. Robotics and Manipulation. Generated and edited images serve as data augmentation for robot learning. RoboSeg (Yuan et al., 2025a) provides 3,800 annotations for robot scene segmentation, enabling physics-aware augmentation that delivers 210% improvement over no-augmentation baselines. Gen2Sim (Katara et al., 2023) evaluates image-to-3D asset generation for robot simulation, demonstrating successful sim-to-real policy transfer. NICE (Pakdamansavoji et al., 2025a) introduces manipulation robustness evaluation across three clutter levels (864 real-world trials), while RoboPearls (Tang et al., 2025) and Masquerade (Lepert et al., 2025a) benchmark scene editing for task generalization, with Masquerade showing that edited human video pre-training achieves 74% success versus 12% for standard pre-training. Creative and Artistic Generation. Layer-Bench (Pu et al., 2025a) evaluates transparent image generation across 1,500 prompts using TIPS and HPSv2 metrics, with user studies (20+ participants) revealing 5759% win rates for the best methods over baselines. Synthetic Media Detection. As generated images become more realistic, detecting them grows critical. DRAGON (Bertazzini et al., 2025a) benchmarks this with 150K images across 25 generative models, revealing robustness challenges: JPEG compression degrades DIRE accuracy from 0.916 to 0.745 at quality factor 30. REALEDIT (Sushko et al., 2025a) focuses on edited image detection in the wild, achieving 4.87% F1 improvement through training on real editing data from Reddit. Industrial Productivity Scenarios. distinct domain has crystallized as image-generation models target professional design workflows. LongCat-Images Poster&SceneBench (Team et al., 2025a) evaluates poster typography and natural scene text on 500 prompts that span signage on textured surfaces and shop fronts under complex lighting. Wan-Image (Mao et al., 2026) introduces an Image Series Generation benchmark covering 13 application categories (fashion, e-commerce, slide presentations, film storyboarding, architecture, and others), evaluating up to 12 still images forming coherent series rather than video, alongside an Interactive Image Editing benchmark targeting precise visual-cue-driven local edits. The shared methodological shift is from relative win-rate scoring to absolute Pass Ratethe proportion of outputs deemed satisfactory in isolationreflecting deployment requirements where model must clear an absolute usability bar regardless of how it ranks against competitors. 5.2.10 Emerging Evaluation Frontiers Despite rapid progress, the current benchmark ecosystem has significant blind spots that fail to capture several emerging capabilities of frontier models. Structured Visual Content. State-of-the-art models can now generate flowcharts, circuit diagrams, chemical structural formulas, and mathematical notationcontent that demands both domain knowledge and precise spatial layout. Yet no established benchmark systematically evaluates the correctness of such outputs. Evaluating generated chemical structure requires verifying valence constraints and bond angles; evaluating flowchart requires checking logical consistency of node connections. These tasks demand domain-specific evaluation protocols that go well beyond prompt-image alignment. Evaluation Methodology Gaps. Current VLM-as-judge pipelines, while scalable, show only moderate correlation with human preferences (typically 0.570.75 Spearman). More concerning, VLM judges can exhibit systematic biases: PSR (Taesiri et al., 2025) finds that when comparing AI-generated edits against human-made edits, VLM evaluators achieve only 0.140.25 Cohens κ agreement with human raters, and show consistent bias toward preferring AI outputslikely because VLMs share similar visual priors with the generative models they evaluate. The field lacks standardized protocols for when VLM evaluation is sufficient versus when human evaluation is necessary, and for how to calibrate automated metrics against human judgments across different cognitive dimensions. 60 Bilingual Coverage as First-Class Dimension. Frontier industrial reports now routinely report Chinese-track results (ChineseWord, GEdit-Bench-CN, OneIG-ZH, LongText-Bench-ZH, CEdit-Bench-CN, REDEdit-Bench-CN), and the cross-language gap is itself diagnosticmodels that lead on English alignment can lag substantially on Chinese (Wu et al., 2025a; Team et al., 2025a, 2026b). Yet most academic benchmarks remain English-only, and there is no widely adopted protocol for how to weight bilingual performance into composite score, leaving systematic blind spot for any model whose deployment markets include Chinese-speaking users. We discuss potential solutions to these evaluation challengesincluding domain-expert-in-the-loop evaluation and compositional correctness verificationin Section 8.

sections.title: 5.2.11 Arena-Based Human Preference Evaluation
sections.content: All benchmarks discussed above evaluate specific cognitive dimensions through automated pipelines, but the ultimate measure of generative model is whether real users prefer its outputs. Arena-based evaluation addresses this by collecting large-scale anonymous human preferences, providing the closest proxy to real-world user satisfaction. Mechanism. Arena platforms present users with blind side-by-side comparison: given prompt, two models generate images whose identities are hidden, and the user votes for the preferred output (or declares tie). The accumulated votes are aggregated into global ranking via the Bradley-Terry model (Chiang et al., 2024), which estimates latent strength parameter ri for each model by maximizing the likelihood of all observed pairwise outcomes: (i j) = exp(ri) exp(ri) + exp(rj) , (20) where the resulting scores are typically reported as Elo ratings. Bootstrap resampling provides confidence intervals to ensure that ranking differences are statistically significant rather than artifacts of sampling noise. Major Platforms. LM Arena (Chiang et al., 2024) extends the widely-used Chatbot Arena infrastructure to text-to-image and image editing leaderboards, with millions of votes across 50+ models, making it the largest-scale visual generation arena. GenAI Arena (Jiang et al., 2024a) provides an open evaluation platform covering text-to-image generation and image editing with Elo-based rankings across 35+ open-source models. Two further platforms have become standard reporting targets in industrial tech reports. Artificial Analysis Image Arena (Artificial Analysis, 2025) runs an independent third-party leaderboard for both text-to-image and image-editing tracks andunlike the otherspublishes inference cost (USD per 1,000 generated images) alongside Elo, exposing the costquality Pareto frontier directly (Seedream et al., 2025; Gao et al., 2025; Cai et al., 2025). Alibaba AI Arena (Wu et al., 2025a), introduced together with Qwen-Image, requires each model to participate in at least 10,000 anonymous pairwise comparisons before reporting and draws votes from over 200 evaluators across professional backgrounds, with explicit dimensions for general image quality, subject accuracy, style adherence, and photographic perspective diversity. Structured Pairwise Human Evaluation. Beyond public arenas, frontier tech reports increasingly run their own structured pairwise or trinary protocols that complement arenas at smaller scale but with controls public platforms cannot enforce. The shared protocol is GoodSameBad (GSB) or its scoring variantsWin Score = (Wins + 0.5BothGood) / Total (Mao et al., 2026), and absolute Pass Ratecomputed over fixed prompt set (typically 1001,000 prompts) with single-run inference per model to forbid cherry-picking, professional evaluators (HunyuanImage 3.0 reports more than 100), and explicit dimension separation along instruction following, consistency, structural integrity, and text-editing performance (Cao et al., 2025; Seedream et al., 2025; Team et al., 2025a, 2026b; Mao et al., 2026). The trade-offs are the inverse of public arenas: smaller scale and self-conducted evaluation introduce risk of selection bias, but controlled inference conditions and pre-registered dimensions yield diagnostic information that public Elo scores concealHunyuanImage 3.0s reported +1.17% relative win rate over Seedream 4.0 and Seedream 4.0s 20% GSB advantage over GPTImage-1 in multi-image editing illustrate the resolution this protocol can achieve. The two evaluation modes are now best read as complementary: public arenas establish overall standing, while structured pairwise protocols isolate specific capability differences. 61 Value and Limitations. Arena rankings serve as the de facto gold standard for overall model comparison they directly reflect aggregated user preferences without relying on proxy metrics or VLM judges. However, they have fundamental limitations that make them complementary to, rather than replacement for, dimension-specific benchmarks. First, arenas cannot scale to serve as development-time evaluation: each data point requires human vote, making rapid iteration impossible. Second, arenas provide no diagnostic informationa model may rank highly overall while failing silently on specific abilities such as text rendering or spatial reasoning. Third, arena rankings can be biased by prompt distribution: the mix of easy and hard prompts submitted by users may not uniformly cover all cognitive dimensions, potentially masking systematic weaknesses. For these reasons, the dimension-specific benchmarks discussed in the preceding sections remain essential for understanding where and why models succeed or fail.

sections.title: 5.3 Infrastructure and Ecosystem: Systematizing Visual Generation
sections.content: As visual generation models transition from traditional U-Nets to Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) and unified autoregressive architectures, the computational bottleneck has fundamentally shifted. The challenge is no longer merely optimizing localized convolutional operators, but managing memory walls, communication overhead, and token scheduling at an unprecedented scale. Modern visual generation has evolved into rigorous systems engineering problem. In this section, we review the underlying infrastructure primitives driving this shift, followed by the open-source ecosystem that democratizes these capabilities. 5.3.1 Scaling the Context: Sequence Parallelism and Load Balancing In the era of DiTs and unified multimodal models (e.g., Transfusion, OmniGen), single high-resolution image or short video is patchified into massive sequences ranging from 105 to over 106 tokens. At this scale, traditional Data Parallelism (DP) and Tensor Parallelism (TP) quickly exhaust GPU memory. Modern infrastructure must incorporate Sequence Parallelism (SP), leveraging communication-efficient mechanisms such as DeepSpeed-Ulysses (Jacobs et al., 2023) or Ring-Attention (Liu et al., 2023a) to distribute the extreme sequence context across multiple devices. Furthermore, unlike the linear nature of text LLMs, visual models frequently process inputs with dynamic resolutions (e.g., jointly training on 5122 and 10242 images). Naive padding strategies can result in up to 50% computational waste. To address this, state-of-the-art visual infrastructure adopts Sequence Packing (or Patch Pack) (Dehghani et al., 2023) techniques, dynamically binning and routing patches of varying resolutions into unified batch to maintain near-optimal Model Flops Utilization (MFU) without compromising spatial integrity. 5.3.2 Orchestrating Visual RL: The Memory Wall in Alignment Post-training alignment via algorithms like PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) or GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) introduces severe memory wall, which is significantly more taxing in the visual domain than in text. Visual RL requires maintaining massive Actor and Reference models in memory while simultaneously caching gradients across multi-step denoising trajectories. To overcome this, infrastructure designs are moving toward highly decoupled architectures. Key system-level solutions include efficient weight synchronization between dedicated training and inference nodes, aggressive CPU offloading for optimizer states, and utilizing high-throughput inference backends (such as vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) or SGLang (Zheng et al., 2024a)) to dramatically accelerate the Actors generation rollout phase. This separation of concerns ensures that GPU compute is not left idle during the computationally expensive reward-scoring and generation steps. Beyond decoupling the generation and scoring stages, recent line of work pushes the boundary of what is jointly optimized even further. PromptRL (Wang et al., 2026) inserts trainable language model as prompt-refinement module inside flow-based RL loop, so that prompt adaptation and generator alignment are co-optimized rather than treated as separate stages. This design illustrates broader trend in visual RL infrastructure: as the system-level memory and throughput bottlenecks are progressively tamed, the scope of end-to-end optimization keeps expanding beyond the generator itself.

sections.title: 5.3.3 Production-Grade Serving and Acceleration
sections.content: Transitioning these foundation models to production shifts the focus toward high-throughput and low-latency serving. Modern visual serving engines are beginning to adopt optimizations originally pioneered for LLMs, but tailored for spatial-temporal data. This includes Continuous Batching (Yu et al., 2022) for asynchronous request handling, advanced KV-Cache management (e.g., adapting PagedAttention (Kwon et al., 2023) to handle cross-modal prefixes in unified generation models), and hardware-aware Operator Fusion via Triton (Tillet et al., 2019) or custom CUDA kernels to minimize memory fragmentation and I/O overhead. concrete realization of this direction is SGLang Diffusion (LMSYS Org, 2026), which extends the SGLang LLM engine with Unified Sequence Parallelism (USP)a composition of Ulysses-SP and Ring-Attention alongside CFG-parallelism and Tensor Parallelism, so that AR and diffusion workloads share single optimized runtime. Reported results show 1.25.9 speedups over standard baselines on representative image and video backbones (Flux, Hunyuan, Wan), delivered behind an OpenAI-compatible API that makes the engine drop-in for production deployments.

sections.title: 5.3.4 The Open-Source Ecosystem and Toolchains
sections.content: Built atop these underlying system primitives is rich ecosystem of modular toolchains that democratizes visual generation research. Rather than writing monolithic training scripts, researchers now rely on specialized frameworks categorized by their operational focus: Omni-modal Training Frameworks: Systems like VeOmni (Ma et al., 2025c) provide model-centric distributed recipes, allowing researchers to easily compose heterogeneous encoders and language backbones across parallel execution environments. RL and Post-Training Toolboxes: UniRL-Zero (Wang et al., 2025c) extends RL beyond singlegenerator optimization to unified models with joint language-model and diffusion-model experts, and integrates broad reward stackaesthetic scorers, fidelity models (ImageReward (Xu et al., 2023a), PickScore (Kirstain et al., 2023), HPS (Wu et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2025e)), and utility metrics (PaddleOCR (Cui et al., 2025) for text rendering, EditReward (Wu et al., 2025e) for instruction following)as pluggable backends so that reward signals can be swapped without touching the training loop. FlowFactory (Ping et al., 2026) complements this with registry-based design that cleanly decouples algorithms, models, and reward functions, supporting methods such as GRPO, DiffusionNFT, and AWM on flow-matching backbones. Base Libraries: At the foundational layer, Hugging Face Diffusers (von Platen et al., 2022) and MMagic (MMagic Contributors, 2023) remain the standard modular toolboxes, exposing schedulers, pipelines, and pretrained building blocks in highly recombinable formatthe essential substrate on which custom research and higher-level frameworks are built. Complementing these, DiffSynthStudio (ModelScope Team, 2024) redesigns the training and inference pipelines for mainstream openweight diffusion model families (FLUX (Labs et al., 2025), Qwen-Image (Wu et al., 2025a), Z-Image (Cai et al., 2025)), adding richer set of training modalities such as FP8 training, end-to-end distillation, two-stage split training, and differential LoRAtargeted at the engineering needs of recent open releases rather than generic schedulers.

sections.title: 6 Applications and Evolving Frontiers
sections.content: Figure 13 summarizes the application landscape discussed in this section. Rather than treating applications as flat list of tasks, the figure organizes them by the type of constraint they impose on visual generation. Fine-grained controllability corresponds to conditional image generation, where auxiliary conditions such as layouts, references, identities, and 3D cues reduce the ambiguity of text prompts. Constructionist paradigms correspond to custom domain adaptation, where generation must obey domain-specific rules such as layout grammar, layered composition, typography, and design conventions. Reasoning-driven editing corresponds to conditional image editing, where the model performs controlled state transitions while preserving unedited content. Finally, the embodied domain covers settings where generation supports physical agents through data augmentation or future-state prediction. This organization makes explicit the common thread across the 63 Spatial & Relational Layout: ReCon (Zhu et al., 2025b), CreatiLayout (Zhang et al., 2025a) MIGLoRA (Qi et al., 2025), MOSAIC (Liu et RoboDreamer (Zhou et al., 2024c) Act-first with visual prediction regularization: U-VLA (Li et al., 2025c), UWM (Zhu et al., 2025a) VideoVLA (Shen et al., 2025b), VPP (Hu et al., 2024b) Embodied Domain (Physical Interaction & Agent Learning) Figure 13 Taxonomy of Applications and Evolving Frontiers in Visual Intelligence. The four branches correspond to the four subsections of this section: conditional image generation, custom domain adaptation, conditional image editing, and embodied-domain generation. section: applications are increasingly defined by verifiable structure, memory, and interaction rather than by visual realism alone. The evolution of visual intelligence, viewed through our hierarchical taxonomy, reflects broader shift from optimizing statistical plausibility toward satisfying increasingly explicit constraints. Early text-to-image synthesis centered on perceptual realism, focusing on reconstructing fine visual details from compressed latent representations. As perceptual fidelity becomes less of the primary bottleneck, recent work has increasingly 64 prioritized controllability, requiring generation to remain faithful not only to natural image statistics but also to structured user intent. Under this view, the generative model is no longer treated solely as probabilistic sampler, but as controllable neural rendering system whose intermediate representations must remain open to intervention. This transition becomes more visible as visual generation moves beyond open-ended synthesis toward settings where spatial layout, temporal coherence, and semantic persistence must be maintained without drifting away from the pretrained image manifold. Following our taxonomy, we organize this progression into three increasingly constrained regimes: inference-time intervention for conditional synthesis, adaptation to domains governed by explicit design rules, and reasoning-driven modification of existing visual content. Together, these regimes reveal common trend: visual generation is gradually evolving from implicit appearance modeling toward structured, programmable control.

sections.title: 6.1 Conditional Image Generation
sections.content: Conditional image generation arises from the persistent under-specification of natural language, whose semantics are often insufficient to determine concrete visual outcomes. To reduce this ambiguity, recent methods augment textual prompts with auxiliary control signals from complementary modalities, ranging from spatial maps and reference images to non-visual signals such as brain activity (Wang et al., 2024b). Early concept personalization methods such as DreamBooth (Ruiz et al., 2023) grounded novel subjects through explicit weight adaptation, establishing strong yet optimization-heavy baseline. Subsequent adapter-based methods, including StyleShot (Gao et al., 2026), improved inference-time flexibility by injecting stylistic cues directly into decoupled cross-attention layers rather than relying only on text. As diffusion backbones scale toward Diffusion Transformers, unified pipelines such as OminiControl (Tan et al., 2025) further extend this trend by routing appearance-specific priors through separated normalization or attention pathways, improving the disentanglement between semantic content and visual presentation. Building on this line of control, identity customization focuses on preserving subject-specific appearance while maintaining text-driven editability. Optimization-based methods, including Textual Inversion (Gal et al., 2022) and LoRA (Hu et al., 2022), showed that personalized concepts can be incorporated effectively into pretrained generators, but their dependence on per-subject tuning often weakens compositional flexibility, editability, and deployment efficiency. This has motivated gradual shift toward tuning-free or near tuning-free personalization. Representative frameworks such as IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023), PhotoVerse (Chen et al., 2023), and FastComposer (Xiao et al., 2025) mitigate identity loss caused by compressed CLIP features by allocating dedicated cross-attention pathways to visual prompts and aligning multi-scale visual representations with text-conditioned generation. Few-shot methods such as PhotoMaker (Li et al., 2024c) further improve robustness by aggregating multiple references into unified identity representation, balancing facial fidelity with semantic controllability. Related efforts that incorporate facial priors or orthogonal projection strategies (Wang et al., 2024a; Guo et al., 2024b; Shi et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2024d; Mou et al., 2025) reinforce persistent identity cues while reducing interference with the models native prompt-following ability. Despite this progress, face-centric personalization transfers only partially to full-body humans and opendomain characters, where large pose changes, self-occlusion, and non-rigid deformation expose the limits of identity representations dominated by facial appearance. In these settings, preserving clothing patterns, body proportions, and cross-view consistency requires stronger spatial correspondence and broader supervision than portrait-focused generation typically provides. Visual Persona (Nam et al., 2025) addresses this gap by leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models to curate dense multi-pose supervision, improving coverage of holistic human structure beyond facial identity. CompleteMe (Tsai et al., 2025) further highlights the importance of reference-guided detail transfer in human image completion, especially for recovering finegrained clothing patterns and accessories under partial observations. In parallel, InstantCharacter (Tao et al., 2025a) introduces reference-aligned spatial attention to track fine-grained traits across diverse articulations. Together, these efforts move conditional generation from rigid subject preservation toward articulated character consistency at the whole-body level. Once subject consistency becomes more reliable, related challenge emerges at the scene level: generation must preserve not only who appears in the image, but also where each semantic element should be placed. This has driven the development of structural control mechanisms that inject explicit geometric cues into the 65 generative backbone. Frameworks such as ControlNet++ (Li et al., 2024a) improve geometric faithfulness through reward-based cycle consistency, while layout-aware methods such as ReCon (Zhu et al., 2025b) rely on visual Chain-of-Thought reasoning to parse compositional instructions and bind semantic entities to designated regions via localized cross-attention masking. When these constraints must remain stable across viewpoints, multi-view frameworks (Xu et al., 2025; Dirik et al., 2025) incorporate 3D-aware priors to maintain epipolar consistency, which is especially important for downstream 3D reconstruction and interactive generation. As identity preservation and spatial control improve, the dominant failure mode shifts again in multientity compositions and long-horizon visual narratives, where independent control signals often interfere with one another and cause feature bleeding across subjects or frames. Recent methods address this challenge by introducing more explicit state management during generation. For example, UniPortrait (He et al., 2025) routes identity tokens to region-specific masks to reduce cross-subject interference, while SpinMeRound (Galanakis et al., 2025) disentangles persistent appearance cues from geometric variation during localized manipulation. These developments extend conditional generation beyond static customization toward more persistent visual state modeling. At the same time, most existing methods still address individual forms of control in isolation, leaving open the broader challenge of unifying identity, structure, and temporal persistence within more compositional control framework.

sections.title: 6.2 Custom Domain Adaptation
sections.content: A recurring challenge in domain adaptation is that the continuous visual priors learned by foundation diffusion models do not naturally align with the discrete structural rules imposed by many specialized applications. Although these models are highly effective at synthesizing naturalistic textures and broad semantic content, they often underrepresent explicit layout grammar, topological regularity, and coordinate-sensitive design constraints. As result, domains that require precise spatial organization expose persistent mismatch between generic visual realism and task-specific correctness. Layout-to-image generation addresses this mismatch by conditioning image generation on explicit spatial specifications such as bounding boxes or segmentation masks. GLIGEN (Li et al., 2023b) introduces layoutconditioned diffusion paradigm that combines textual descriptions with designated spatial regions via gated self-attention, enabling fine-grained control over entity position and attributes. InstanceDiffusion (Wang et al., 2024d) broadens the spatial interface to support diverse formatspoints, scribbles, boxes, and maskswithin unified fusion architecture. MIGC (Zhou et al., 2024b) enhances multi-instance control by decomposing generation into per-entity subtasks and leveraging instance-specific attention to better regulate quantity, position, and attributes. CreatiLayout (Zhang et al., 2025a) strengthens region-entity alignment through siamese multimodal mechanism that jointly encodes layout and visual features with Diffusion Transformer. HybridLayout (Wu et al., 2025d) further questions the assumption that strong layout adherence for modern Diffusion Transformers requires millions of densely annotated regional prompts. Its key insight is to decompose layout control into two sequential stages: first grounding anonymous layouts so the model learns where objects should appear, and then applying lightweight semantic refinement so the model learns what each designated region should contain. By combining this hybrid supervision strategy with low-resolution regional tokens and region-wise diffusion loss, the method achieves strong spatial fidelity with dramatically fewer semantic layout annotations while preserving better visual aesthetics. DreamRenderer (Zhou et al., 2025) achieves training-free multi-instance attribute control by selectively applying hard attribute binding at critical transformer layers. Collectively, these advances transform foundation diffusion models into controllable generative systems capable of precise, instance-level semantic and positional regulation under increasingly data-efficient supervision. Beyond explicit layout conditioning, an increasingly relevant direction is layered image generation, which represents an image as set of compositional layers rather than single flattened output. Compared with bounding boxes or segmentation masks alone, layered representations expose richer structural information, including foreground-background decomposition, occlusion ordering, transparency, and layer-wise editability. This makes them particularly suitable for domains where visual elements must remain independently controllable after synthesis, such as graphic design, advertising creatives, interface assets, and document-like media. Existing approaches generally follow two paradigms. Simultaneous generation methods generate multiple layers jointly within unified model, improving global coherence and cross-layer consistency during synthesis, as seen in Text2Layer (Zhang et al., 2023b), LayerDiff (Huang et al., 2024), ART (Pu et al., 2025b), PrismLayer (Chen 66 et al., 2025e), and Qwen-Image-Layered (Yin et al., 2025b). In contrast, sequential generation methods decompose the process into progressive layer prediction or transparent-image reconstruction, which offers greater flexibility for iterative composition and editing, as explored by LayerDiffuse (Zhang and Agrawala, 2024), COLE (Jia et al., 2023), OpenCOLE (Inoue et al., 2024), and LayerD (Suzuki et al., 2025). From the perspective of domain adaptation, layered generation can be viewed as stronger structural abstraction than conventional layout control: it specifies not only where content should appear, but also how visual elements are separated, ordered, and recombined. As such, it extends controllable generation from spatial alignment toward editable compositional reasoning. Despite these spatial control capabilities, many specialized domains impose additional constraints beyond In commercial media and poster generation, for instance, generation must jointly layout arrangement. satisfy typography placement, visual balance, and compositional aesthetics. Recent frameworks such as PosterVerse (Liu et al., 2026) and PosterGen (Zhang et al., 2025i) address this by reformulating generation as modular planning problem, using Large Language Models to translate design intent into explicit layout variables and refine them through iterative visual feedback. Going further, CreatiDesign (Zhang et al., 2025b) unifies heterogeneous design conditionsreference images, layouts, and rendered textwithin single model through multimodal attention mask mechanism, enabling fine-grained control over each sub-condition while maintaining overall compositional harmony. As these pipelines become more structured, second line of work focuses on aligning generated outputs with domain-specific quality measures and content regimes. BizGen (Peng et al., 2025), for example, extends visual generation from sentence-level text rendering to article-level business content such as infographics and slides, combining scalable dense-layout data construction with layout-guided cross-attention for ultra-dense region-wise rendering. PosterOmni (Chen et al., 2026), PosterCraft (Chen et al., 2025j), and POSTAAG (Chen et al., 2025b) further combine task-aware distillation, reward modeling, and feedback-driven refinement to discourage failures such as text-background occlusion, hierarchy violations, or spatial imbalance. Rather than treating background synthesis and foreground arrangement as separate problems, these frameworks optimize them jointly so that visual content and layout evolve consistently. In contrast to these training-heavy solutions, ICCustom (Li et al., 2025d) shows that part of this domain alignment can also be induced at inference time through multimodal in-context learning, where interleaved visual and textual exemplars steer generation without requiring domain-specific parameter updates. Beyond scene-level composition, specialized domains also demand precise rendering of local symbolic content, where approximate visual resemblance is no longer sufficient. This requirement is especially evident in text generation, where the granularity of language tokenization does not align well with the stroke-level geometry needed for accurate glyph synthesis. As result, standard diffusion pipelines often produce visually plausible yet semantically incorrect characters. Although dedicated text-rendering architectures such as GlyphByT5-v2 (Liu et al., 2024c) have substantially improved typographic quality, more recent efforts increasingly emphasize localized and decoupled control over character structure. Segmentation-conditioned methods address this issue by injecting explicit typographical constraints into spatially bounded regions. ControlText (Jiang et al., 2025a) and ReChar (Yang et al., 2025c), for example, modulate region-specific cross-attention so that glyph generation is anchored to designated masks and local structural priors (Lu et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2023a). In parallel, methods such as CharGen (Ma et al., 2024a) and UMText (Ma et al., 2026) revisit the representation problem itself by replacing generic language tokenizers with character-aware encoders, thereby strengthening the alignment between symbolic input and visual realization. Together, these approaches shift the problem from coarse text-conditioned synthesis toward more faithful character-level rendering. Recent work further extends text rendering beyond planar overlays toward physically grounded generation in spatially structured environments. Frameworks such as WordCon (Shi et al., 2025b) and Beyond (Luo et al., 2025a) introduce self-guidance mechanisms that extract intermediate geometric signals, including depth and surface normals, from the generative process and feed them back as structural conditions. This design enables text to inherit explicit 3D geometry and material-aware appearance, making it more consistent with scene lighting, viewpoint, and physical support surfaces. Overall, these developments show that domain adaptation is moving from broad stylistic transfer toward rule-aware generation under both global and local constraints. Yet much of this progress remains tied to narrowly defined domains, with limited support for more general, 67 reusable control abstractions across tasks.

sections.title: 6.3 Conditional Image Editing
sections.content: Conditional image editing reframes generative modeling from open-ended synthesis to constrained state transition problem, where the desired output must satisfy editing instructions while preserving the source image wherever no change is required. This creates persistent tension between plasticity, namely the ability to modify targeted attributes, and stability, namely the preservation of identity, structure, and context outside the edited region. Standard diffusion models often struggle with this balance because globally shared attention can entangle edited and unedited content, causing local instructions to propagate unexpectedly across the image. Recent work therefore places increasing emphasis on structured control, decomposition, and consistency preservation to regulate how edits are introduced into the latent trajectory. prominent direction addresses complex and multi-step edits by decomposing high-level instructions into more explicit intermediate operations. Frameworks such as X-Planner (Yeh et al., 2025) and MIRA (Zeng et al., 2025b) employ Multi-modal Large Language Models to parse abstract user intent into sequentially executable subgoals, making the editing process more transparent and controllable across multiple stages. Related methods, including ReasonEdit (Yin et al., 2025a) and LegoEdit (Jia et al., 2025), further separate semantic reasoning from low-level rendering, thereby reducing ambiguity in how instructions are grounded into pixel-space transformations. At more symbolic level, program-like editing formulations (Hu et al., 2025) translate natural language into executable operations, allowing image transitions to be governed by explicit logical structure rather than by diffusion dynamics alone. While instruction decomposition improves semantic clarity, edit quality still depends critically on whether the original visual manifold can be preserved outside the target region. To reduce unwanted structural drift, recent methods introduce explicit consistency modules that regularize the denoising trajectory under spatial and quantitative constraints. IMAGHarmony (Shen et al., 2025a) and TBStarEdit (Fang et al., 2025b), for example, strengthen local-global consistency so that modifications remain concentrated around the intended edit scope. For scenarios requiring even finer control, physically grounded methods such as PhysicEdit (Zhao et al., 2026) and layered editing frameworks (Yang et al., 2026) decompose the image into semantically separable strata, enabling foreground manipulation while preserving the surrounding context more explicitly. The precision of such methods is increasingly examined by dedicated benchmarks (Ghazanfari et al., 2025), which evaluate not only whether the intended content is changed correctly, but also whether the remaining image stays stable. As editing systems move closer to interactive use, different bottleneck emerges at deployment time: per-image optimization becomes increasingly difficult to reconcile with responsive applications. This has motivated recent work on weight-free or feed-forward editing paradigms that retain instruction following while reducing adaptation cost. Methods based on visual in-context learning (Zhang et al., 2025h) and task-aware functional representations (Mao et al., 2025; Ma et al., 2025b) show that robust editing behavior can be induced without expensive gradient updates. Complementary architectural efforts further streamline the inference path toward high-fidelity, near single-step editing (Liu et al., 2025f; Yao et al., 2025). Together, these advances move conditional image editing from offline optimization toward more deployable visual manipulation. However, most existing methods still optimize individual aspects of editing, such as planning, preservation, or efficiency, separately, rather than treating editing as part of unified control process that jointly reasons about intent, locality, and state consistency.

sections.title: 6.4 Embodied Domain
sections.content: Embodied applications of visual generation and editing extend beyond static synthesis to settings where visual outputs interact with or support physical agents and environments. In these contexts, generative models serve as tools for augmenting data and for modeling visual state transitions under control, enabling downstream tasks in robotics, simulation, and agent learning (Mei et al., 2026; Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a). We categorize embodied applications into two primary modalities: 1) Visual Data Generation and Augmentation for Agents, and 2) Visual Prediction for Embodied Interaction.

sections.title: Embodied Applications of Visual Generation
sections.content: Data Generation & Augmentation

sections.title:
Prediction for
Interaction
sections.content: Data construction Datasets, simulation Predict-then-act Future prediction Transfer & editing Cross-embodiment adaptation Prediction-regularized Auxiliary signal Diversification Expanded behaviors Hybrid Joint modeling Role Coverage & generalization Role Dynamics & temporal structure Figure 14 Embodied applications of visual generation can be organized into two complementary roles. The first uses generation and augmentation to expand embodied training data through construction, transfer, and diversification, thereby improving coverage and generalization. The second uses visual prediction to support embodied interaction by modeling future observations, regularizing action learning, and capturing environment dynamics. 6.4.1 Visual Data Generation and Augmentation for Agents In many embodied systems, the limited availability of diverse interaction data inhibits both learning and generalization (Khazatsky et al., 2024; ONeill et al., 2024; Walke et al., 2023). Visual generation can mitigate this by synthesizing additional data that preserves both visual realism and physically plausible state diversity (Chen et al., 2024a; Lepert et al., 2025b; Li et al., 2025a; Lepert et al., 2025a; Fan et al., 2026; Song et al., 2025a). Representative uses include: Trajectory Diversification: Given an initial state (e.g., an image depicting an object configuration or scene), generate multiple plausible future visual sequences that correspond to different action sequences or environmental perturbations (Zhu et al., 2024a; Fu et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2024c; Barcellona et al., 2024). This expanded dataset enhances robustness during policy learning or control refinement (Zhu et al., 2024a; Zhou et al., 2024c). Cross-Morphology Editing: Edit existing perception data to reflect alternative agent embodiments (e.g., replacing human arm with robotic manipulator or substituting object appearances) without collecting new real-world samples (Chen et al., 2024a; Lepert et al., 2025b; Li et al., 2025a; Lepert et al., 2025a; Fan et al., 2026; Song et al., 2025a). Such editing supports transfer learning and evaluation under varied agent morphologies (Chen et al., 2024a; Li et al., 2025a). These applications treat visual generation and editing as data augmentation mechanisms that enrich interaction datasets with minimal real-world collection cost, while preserving semantic and physical coherence. 6.4.2 Visual Prediction for Embodied Interaction prominent embodied application of visual generation is to support decision-making through predictive modeling of future observations under interaction (Zhu et al., 2024a; Hu et al., 2024b; Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a). In this setting, visual generation is used to anticipate how the environment may evolve from the current observation, providing informative context for downstream action prediction (Zhu et al., 2024a; Hu et al., 2024b). widely adopted approach first predicts multiple plausible future visual states given the current observation, and then infers executable actions for the agent based on these predicted futures (Zhao et al., 2025; Tian et al., 2024b; Du et al., 2023; Ko et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2025a). By explicitly modeling prospective environment evolution, this formulation enables the model to reason over alternative outcomes and long-term consequences before committing to actions (Du et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2025a; Zhou et al., 2024c). Learning accurate visual dynamics is typically the more challenging component, while action inference is comparatively simpler once future states are available (Zhu et al., 2024a; Tian et al., 2024b; Hu et al., 2024b). An alternative formulation reverses this order: models first predict actions directly from current visual inputs, and subsequently generate future visual observations as an auxiliary objective (Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a; Liang et al., 2025; Shen et al., 2025b; Hu et al., 2024b). In this case, visual prediction does not serve as an explicit intermediate for planning, but instead provides additional supervision that encourages temporal consistency, physical plausibility, and structured representations aligned with environment dynamics (Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a). These two formulations reflect different design choices rather than fundamentally different objectives. Both couple visual generation with action reasoning, differing mainly in whether future visual prediction is treated as primary decision-support signal or as auxiliary regularization (Tian et al., 2024b; Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a). In practice, hybrid designs that interleave or jointly optimize visual prediction and action inference are also common (Li et al., 2025c; Zhu et al., 2025a; Liang et al., 2025; Shen et al., 2025b). Overall, visual prediction in embodied interaction functions as mechanism for encoding environment dynamics and temporal structure, enabling agents to make informed decisions in sequential, interactive settings rather than producing visually accurate frames in isolation (Mei et al., 2026; Hu et al., 2024b; Li et al., 2025c).

sections.title: 7 Assessment for the Future: Stress Testing the Limits
sections.content: The five-level taxonomy introduced in Section 2 claims that visual intelligence progresses from atomic generation (L1) through conditional generation (L2), in-context generation (L3), and agentic generation (L4) toward world-modeling generation (L5). But how far have current frontier models actually advanced along this trajectory? Standard benchmarksFID, CLIP-Score, isolated prompt-following metricspredominantly evaluate L1 and L2 capabilities: whether the generated image is realistic and whether it matches the text. They tell us little about whether models can solve spatial puzzles (L2 boundary), maintain identity across multi-turn edits (L3), self-correct through reasoning (L4), or simulate physical causality (L5). To probe these higher-level capabilities, we complement benchmark-based evaluation with series of in-the-wild stress tests: carefully designed tasks that target the boundary conditions of each level. Each dimension below is explicitly mapped to the level it primarily challenges, as summarized in Table 8. This design ensures that the stress tests serve not merely as anecdotal failure cases, but as structured evidence for where the current frontier sits within our taxonomy.

sections.title: 7.1 Methodology: From Benchmarks to "In-the-Wild" Scenarios
sections.content: Generator and outputs. Each figure caption indicates the generator used (Nano Banana or GPT-Image-2). The two systems are evaluated under identical prompts and settings, enabling direct cross-system comparison without changing the task definitions. Paper figures as embedded probes. Several explanatory figures in this work are themselves produced with frontier image-generation models under expert-authored descriptions. We do not treat these illustrations as formal benchmarks, because their prompts, selection process, and revision decisions are coupled to the authors communicative goals. Nevertheless, they provide complementary form of real-world case study: when model is asked to generate semantically correct roadmap figure, taxonomy diagram, system schematic, or scientifically constrained visual explanation from dense expert specification, it must simultaneously follow domain semantics, visual grammar, layout constraints, and readability requirements. Success therefore offers evidence of generation capability beyond photorealism, while failure exposes the same issues studied in the stress tests belowmissing relations, visually plausible but scientifically inaccurate structure, weak symbolic grounding, or unstable text rendering. For this reason, AI-assisted figures used in the paper should be read as embedded probes of structured visual generation, with final correctness still requiring expert verification.

sections.title: 7.2 Dimension I: Spatial Structuring & Layout Precision
sections.content: Primary Level Tested: L2 (Conditional Generation) Can the model faithfully translate spatial constraints into precise geometric arrangements? Core Ability: Can the model follow strict geometric constraints? (Count, Ratio, Position).

sections.title: 7.2.1 Case Study I: The Jigsaw Puzzle Challenge (Geometric Rigidity vs. Generative Hallucination)
sections.content: To strictly evaluate the models capability in Spatial Structuring and Conservation of Content, we designed the "Jigsaw Reconstruction" task. Unlike standard image generation where creativity is encouraged, this task requires the model to act as rigid solver: it must reassemble visual fragments into coherent whole based strictly on edge matching and texture continuity, without adding or removing visual information. Experimental Setup. We employed "Visual Reconstruction Specialist" system prompt (see Appendix for full prompt), instructing the model to strictly adhere to "Seamless Integrity" and "Strict Edge Matching." Input: fragmented view of hot air balloons (puzzle_input.jpg), containing disjointed puzzle pieces with significant spatial gaps and randomized overlaps. Ground Truth: high-resolution photograph of multiple hot air balloons against clear blue sky (puzzle_gt.jpg). Challenge: The model must recognize the discrete nature of the pieces and spatially rearrange them to restore the original topology. (a) Input: Scrambled Fragments (b) Model Output (c) Ground Truth Figure 15 Failure Mode in Spatial Reconstruction. While the model successfully recovers the semantic theme (balloons in the sky), it fails the geometric test. Instead of solving the puzzle logic (matching tabs and slots), it "hallucinates" plausible-looking but structurally incoherent image, blending distinct pieces into amassed artifacts. Generated by Nano Banana. Analysis of Results. As shown in Figure 15, the result highlights critical limitation in current foundation models: Semantic Success vs. Geometric Failure: The model correctly identifies the semantic context (colorful balloons, blue sky). It attempts to group similar textures (blue pieces together, striped pieces together). The "Hallucination" Trap: Instead of performing rigid transformations (rotation/translation) on the pixel fragments, the model utilizes its generative prior to "dream" solution. It generates new balloon-like textures to fill the gaps rather than stitching the existing edges. Lack of Object Permanence: The input constraints explicitly forbade "hallucinating" content. However, the output shows significant artifacts where multiple pieces are fused into non-existent geometries, and original edge details (the "tabs" and "blanks" of puzzle pieces) are dissolved rather than utilized for alignment. Insight. This case study demonstrates that even with strong "Agentic" prompt, current visual models operate primarily on Probabilistic Correlation (Level 1/2) rather than Causal/Physical Logic (Level 5). They prioritize making the image look like completed puzzle (semantic consistency) over actually solving the puzzle (spatial consistency). True spatial intelligence requires the ability to treat image patches as rigid bodies with permanent properties, capability currently lacking in standard diffusion pipelines. (a) Isometric tile map (b) Metro-system map Figure 16 Spatial structuring under explicit layout constraints. Both examples are generated by GPT-Image-2 from prompts with discrete spatial or topological requirements. The outputs are visually polished and closely match the intended design styles: professional isometric tactical map in Figure 16a and clean vector-style metro map in Figure 16b. However, both cases reveal subtle but important failures in constraint execution, including coordinate-level object shifts in the tile map and graph-topology violations in the metro map. 7.2.2 Case Study II: The Metro Map Challenge (Topological Plausibility vs. Constraint Verification) Prompt. Create professional metro-system map for fictional city. It must contain four colored lines: Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow. There are exactly 18 station names. Three stations are transfer stations shared by exactly two lines, and one central station is shared by all four lines. The Red and Blue lines cross twice but share only one station; the Green line forms loop; the Yellow line branches once after its third station. Use clean vector-map aesthetics, readable station labels, legend, and no geographic background. The topology must be internally consistent. Result. As shown in Figure 16b, the generated map is visually polished and satisfies several high-level requirements. It correctly contains 18 stations in total, the Green Line forms loop, and there are three transfer stations shared by exactly two lines. Nevertheless, several key topological constraints are violated. Most notably, the prompt requires one central station shared by all four lines, but the generated central station (Grand Central) is shared by only three linesRed, Blue, and Yellowwhile the Green Line does not pass through it. In addition, the constraint that the Red and Blue lines should cross twice but share only one station is not satisfied, since the generated map shows only one such crossing. Finally, the Yellow Line branching rule is also incorrect: the branch is not placed after its third station as specified. Insight. This case highlights central limitation of current text-to-image systems in spatially structured generation: they can often reproduce the visual grammar of structured diagram, but fail to enforce the underlying discrete relational constraints. The model captures the appearance of professional metro map color-coded routes, readable station labels, loop-like geometry, transfer stations, and legendyet it does not 72 faithfully preserve the graph-level topology required by the prompt. In other words, the output is visually plausible but not topologically valid. particularly revealing aspect of this example is the contrast between generation and post-hoc verification. In this case, GPT-Image-2 spent 13m15s thinking before generation, yet still produced map that violates multiple explicit constraints. By contrast, when the generated image and the original prompt were provided jointly to GPT 5.5 with the simple query What parts of the image do not match the prompt?, the model identified all major mismatches in only 9s. This suggests that constructing constraint-satisfying visual artifact is substantially harder than checking an already rendered artifact against symbolic requirements. More broadly, long deliberation during image generation does not necessarily translate into reliable constraint satisfaction.

sections.title: 7.2.3 Case Study III: The Isometric Tile Map Challenge (Coordinate Grounding vs. Visual Plausibility)
sections.content: Prompt. Generate clean isometric game map made of exactly 88 square tiles. Use strict isometric angle and make every tile edge align perfectly. Place river occupying tiles B2B6, stone bridge crossing the river at B4D4, three houses at F2, F5, and G6, one marketplace at D7, and 2-tile-high hill in the northwest corner. Add small legend showing water, road, house, bridge, and hill. The result should look like professional tactical board-game map, not fantasy painting. Result. As shown in Figure 16a, the generated map is visually convincing and successfully captures the intended style of professional isometric tactical board-game map. The overall grid structure is clean, the isometric viewpoint is mostly consistent, tile boundaries are well aligned, and the terrain assets are rendered with coherent lighting and texture. The legend is also correctly included, covering the requested categories: water, road, house, bridge, and hill. Several high-level spatial constraints are also broadly satisfied: the river, bridge, northwest hill, and one of the houses are placed in way that is largely consistent with the prompt. However, the result still fails on precise coordinate grounding. The houses specified at F5 and G6 are shifted to nearby but incorrect cells, approximately F6 and G7. Similarly, the marketplace requested at D7 is placed lower than expected, closer to D8. These errors are visually subtle because the map remains coherent and game-like, but they violate the rule-based placement constraints that define the task. Insight. This case illustrates recurring failure mode in spatially structured generation: the model can reproduce the visual grammar of tile-based map, but does not reliably execute the underlying coordinate program. Isometric grids impose discrete symbolic structure, where each object must be bound to specific cell. Yet the model appears to treat coordinates such as F5 and G6 as soft layout cues rather than exact addresses. As result, it produces perceptually plausible map while committing off-by-one placement errors. More broadly, this suggests that current text-to-image models still lack robust internal mechanism for translating symbolic spatial specifications into deterministic geometric layouts. Their strength lies in synthesizing coherent visual patterns, not in maintaining verifiable grid state throughout generation. Similar to the metro-map case in Figure 16b, the failure is not aesthetic but procedural: the image looks correct at glance, yet the discrete spatial relations are not fully satisfied. This again reveals gap between generation and verification. multimodal reasoning model such as GPT 5.5 can readily identify these coordinate mismatches after seeing the image and prompt, while the image generator itself struggles to prevent them during generation.

sections.title: 7.3 Dimension II: Physical Reasoning & Causal Fidelity
sections.content: Primary Level Tested: L5 (World-Modeling Generation) Can the model predict what would physically happen under intervention, not merely what looks plausible? Core Ability: Does the model understand "How the world works" (Gravity, Lighting, Crowd Dynamics)? Pure physical reasoning, however, is not always evaluated in purely textual form. Some of the most revealing cases arise when the model must read visually presented physics problem, solve it, and then write the derivation back onto the same image. Such tasks lie at the boundary between Physical Reasoning 73 Input: Floating (a) State Figure 17 Causal Transition in Fluid Dynamics. The model successfully transitions from surface-level state (a) to submerged state (c) upon counterfactual prompt. Note the addition of micro-bubbles and shifted refractive caustics, indicating an implicit understanding of fluid interaction. Generated by Nano Banana. (c) Counterfactual: Sinking (b) Analytical Explainer & Causal Fidelity and Visual-Textual Integration & Logic. In this section, we place our physics-exam case primarily under Dimension III, because the decisive challenge is not only whether the model can infer the correct physical law, but whether it can close the full OCRreasoningrendering loop without breaking the visual structure of the page. 7.3.1 Case Study I: Fluid Dynamics and Counterfactual Buoyancy To probe the models grasp of World-Modeling Generation, we designed two-stage stress test involving floating object and counterfactual physical intervention. Experimental Setup. We utilized high-resolution image of citrus slice floating on water surface as the anchor. The evaluation was split into two instructions: Instruction (Analytical Rendering): "Create an illustrated explainer detailing the physics of the fluid dynamics caught in this image." This tests Visual-Textual Integration (Dimension III) and the ability to map physical concepts (e.g., surface tension, Archimedes principle) onto visual substrate. Instruction (Counterfactual State Transition): "What if the orange piece sinks down the water?" This requires the model to move beyond "Stochastic Plausibility" toward "Causal Simulation," predicting the resulting change in lighting, bubbles, and displacement. Observed Behavior. As shown in Figure 17, the model demonstrates sophisticated "VLM-first, renderersecond" pipeline. In the analytical explainer, it successfully generates vector-like annotations and labels that align with the spatial coordinates of the object. More critically, in the sinking scenario, the model does not merely lower the objects vertical position; it introduces causal artifacts such as trailing air bubbles and altered light refraction consistent with submerged environment. Insight. This case suggests that frontier models are beginning to bridge the gap between L2 (Conditional Generation) and L5 (World-Modeling Generation). While the "Thinking Trace" often reveals redundant search for consistency, the final output indicates that "Reasoning-conditioned document editing" is viable path toward agentic visual systems that understand "how the world works". 7.3.2 Case Study II: Action-Conditioned Navigation and Predictive World Modeling We further evaluate the models capacity for predictive world modeling within an embodied driving context. This requires the model to predict future visual states conditioned on specific agent actions or high-kinetic counterfactuals. (a) Scenario A: Initial Intersection State (b) Predicted State: Perspective Shift (c) Scenario B: Highway Anchor (d) Predicted State: High Speed (e) Counterfactual: Collision Figure 18 Action-Conditioned Causal Prediction. The model successfully simulates visual markers of high-speed travel (motion blur) and the structural consequences of physical crash, including metal deformation and fragmentation, indicating significant progress toward Level 5 World Simulation. Generated by Nano Banana. Experimental Setup. The model is presented with two navigation-based challenges: Scenario (Intersection Turning): From first-person view at signalized intersection, the model is asked if turn is possible and to generate the resulting view. This tests the preservation of landmarks and dynamic actors (pedestrians) across 3D coordinate shift. Scenario (High-Speed Collision): Given highway scene, the model is prompted with severe counterfactual: "What if further speed up and crash into the car ahead?". Insight. While the model demonstrates high structural accuracy in the collision test, its failure to explicitly flag immediate safety constraints (e.g., the pedestrian currently in the crosswalk during Scenario A) highlights critical gap between "Visual Prediction" and "Grounded Causal Reasoning". This reinforces the finding that current systems often prioritize semantic plausibility over robust causal competence. 7.3.3 Case Study III: Task-Oriented Action Grounding and Robotic Manipulation To move from passive physical simulation toward proactive, goal-directed behavior, we evaluate the models capacity for Generative Action Grounding. This task requires the model to translate high-level functional intent (e.g., grasp the cup) into visually and physically coherent action plan. Experimental Setup. The model is presented with robot workspace featuring robotic end-effector and target object (a white cup) on flat surface. We issue the prompt: If have robot arm with gripper and need to grasp the cup, how should the gripper grasp the cup on the table. Visualize it. The models response is evaluated based on its ability to propose gripper orientation that accounts for the objects geometry and the physics of contact. Analysis of Physical Reasoning. The results in Figure 19 reveal that the model possesses an emergent World Model for manipulation. In the schematic examples (a-b), the model maintains Object Permanence and correctly identifies the cylindrical affordance of the cup. In the photorealistic rendering (c), the model avoids the common failure of interpenetrating the mesh; instead, it wraps the robotic fingers around the mug in way that suggests an understanding of friction and surface normals. 75 (a) Input: Mug to grasp (b) Input: Robot gripper (c) Photorealistic Execution Figure 19 Generative Action Grounding for Robotic Manipulation. (a-b) The input mug to grasp and the robot gripper. (c) The model successfully synthesizes high-fidelity anthropomorphic grasp, demonstrating an implicit understanding of Contact Manifold dynamics and force-closure. Generated by Nano Banana. Insight. This case study highlights that advanced VLMs can act as Visual Policy Proposals. While the model is not outputting raw joint torques, its ability to generate visually valid future stateconditioned on functional goalmarks significant step toward Agentic Visual Intelligence. It demonstrates that the model is not just stochastic parrot of pixels, but system capable of running mental simulation of physical contact to guide its generative process. 7.3.4 Case Study IV: Spatiotemporal Trajectory Synthesis for Multi-Step Tasks To evaluate the models capacity for long-horizon physical reasoning, we move beyond static pose estimation to Spatiotemporal Trajectory Synthesis. In this task, the model must visualize multi-step sequence of actions that transition scene from "source" state to "target" state, maintaining causal logic throughout the motion. Experimental Setup. The model is provided with workspace containing green spoon and wooden bowl (spoon_input.png). We issue trajectory-based prompt: Generate the trajectory to put the green spoon into the wooden bowl as sequence of images from the same camera. This requires the model to simulate continuous path that respects the physical constraints of both the robotic end-effector and the environment. Analysis of Spatiotemporal Consistency. The generated sequence in Figure 20 reveals two critical competencies: 1. Kinematic Logic: The robotic arms movement follows natural arc, indicating an implicit understanding of joint constraints and end-effector orientation. 2. Containment Awareness: In the final frame, the spoon is correctly occluded by the rim of the bowl, signifying that the model understands the 3D volume of the container and the physics of the inside relationship. However, there is an obvious failure in the first two frames of the generated trajectory, where the spoon orientation is wrong, indicating that more research is needed for visual detail consistencies. Insight. This case study highlights the emergence of Visual Action Planning. By successfully rendering temporal sequence that adheres to Newtonian intuition, the model proves it can act as simulator for high-level robotic tasks. However, remaining challenge for Agentic Intelligence is ensuring these trajectories are not just visually plausible but also kinematically executable in real-world control systems. 7.3.5 Case Study V: Video Re-rendering with Functional Causal Failure To evaluate the limits of high-fidelity spatiotemporal consistency in video-to-video editing, we conducted real-world multi-frame stress test requiring complex identity replacement: transforming human performing 76 (a) Input: Initial State (b) Output: Generated trajectory Figure 20 Sequential Trajectory Generation. The model demonstrates an ability to synthesize physically plausible path through time. It maintains strict Identity Persistence for the spoon and correctly predicts the spatial containment relationship required for the task. This illustrates transition toward Level 5 intelligence, where visual generation serves as proxy for physical planning. Generated by Nano Banana. sequential kitchen task into consistent humanoid robot while preserving the trajectory and functional intent. Experimental Setup. The model is presented with consecutive four-frame sequence from kitchen camera (human_input.jpg), featuring human performing complex manipulation tasks around stove and sink. We issue task-conditioned visual editing prompt: Replace the human in those sequential frames with humanoid. The robot must be consistent along frames. The evaluation criteria are (a) identity persistence of the humanoid, and (b) preservation of the original functional action and its causal consequences. Observation and Analysis. As depicted in Figure 21, the model successfully replaces the human with single, visually detailed, and physically plausible humanoid that maintains identity persistence across all frames. However, significant divergence occurs regarding Functional Causal Persistence. In frame 2 of the source sequence (Figure 21a, top right), the human is clearly pouring from container into pot. The model, while pathing the robot to the same location, fails to render the act of pouring or simulate its consequences (e.g., fluid dynamics, maintained pose). The action is reduced to semantic gesture of interaction, rather than simulation. Insight. This real-world example exposes the gap between Pixel-space Control and Physical Simulation. The model excels at L2/L3 (Conditional and In-Context Generation) but remains far from L5 (World-Modeling Generation). It treats sequential visual action as chain of static texturally consistent states rather than continuous event with logical consequences. The failure of the pouring action highlights that while visual generation is reaching high maturity, grounding these generations in robust physical and logical world models is persistent bottleneck for agentic visual systems that must operate safely and functionally in dynamic environments. 7.3.6 Case Study VI: Irreversible State Transitions and Internal Material Consistency To push the limits of World-Modeling Generation, we evaluate the models ability to represent irreversible physical transformationsspecifically Material Removal and Subdivision. These tasks require the model to 77 (a) Original Human Sequence: Multi-Step Causal Tasks (e.g., Pouring Action in Frame 2) (b) Edited Humanoid Sequence: Plausible Identity but Causal Action Failure (Missing Pouring Simulation) Figure 21 Video Re-rendering and Causal Divergence. The model achieves high controllable creativity (Level 2) by consistently rendering single humanoid robot along the original spatiotemporal path. However, it fails to simulate the functional grounding of the task; the pouring action present in the source (a, Frame 2) disappears and has no causal consequence in the edited sequence (b), demonstrating decoupling of visual fidelity and causal world simulation (Level 5). Generated by Nano Banana. predict the internal structure of an object that is hidden in the initial state, testing the depth of its volumetric world knowledge. Experimental Setup. The model is provided with whole zucchini and rainbow carrot (veg_input.jpg). We issued two goal-conditioned instructions: (1) Generate the goal image of cutting zucchini, and (2) Generate the goal image of peeling rainbow carrot. successful simulation must preserve the objects identity while correctly rendering the internal flesh and resulting debris (slices/shavings). Analysis of Volumetric Reasoning. The generated images in Figure 22 reveal that the model treats the vegetables as 3D volumes rather than 2D sprites. The transition from (a) to (b) and (c) to (d) involves significant change in geometry. The model accurately simulates the Internal Material Properties: the zucchini slices have logically consistent diameter and interior color, while the carrot shavings exhibit the correct thickness and curvature expected from manual peeler. Insight. This case study highlights the models capacity for Counterfactual State Synthesis. By accurately imagining the "inside" of an object based on its "outside," the model moves toward Level 5 intelligence. However, the exact arrangement of the slices in (b) remains somewhat stochastic; truly agentic model would need to ground these transformations in rigid kinematic path (e.g., the specific movement of knife), bridging the gap between visual plausibility and physical execution. (a) Initial State: Original Zucchini (b) Goal State: Cut Zucchini (c) Initial State: Original Carrot (d) Goal State: Peeled Rainbow Carrot Figure 22 Irreversible Material Transformation. The model demonstrates an advanced understanding of object interiority. In (b), it correctly renders the internal seeds and texture of the zucchini. In (d), it maintains the specific color gradient of the rainbow carrot in the resulting shavings, showcasing high Causal Identity Persistence across state changes. Generated by Nano Banana.

sections.title: 7.4 Dimension III: Visual-Textual Integration & Logic
sections.content: Primary Level Tested: L4 (Agentic Generation) Can the model read, reason, and write back onto the same visual substrate in coherent closed-loop workflow? Core Ability: The fusion of OCR (Reading), Reasoning (Solving), and Rendering (Writing). 7.4.1 Case Study I: Solving Physics Exam Directly on the Image To probe whether frontier model can truly read, think, and write back on the same visual substrate, we constructed physics-tutoring case in which the input is Chinese Gaokao-style electromagnetism problem and the output must be an annotated solution image rather than plain text. The user prompt explicitly casts the model as Visual Physics Solver & Annotator and requires an end-to-end workflow: parse the question and diagram, identify the governing physical laws, derive the equations, perform the calculations, and overlay the step-by-step solution directly onto the image or an extended whitespace region. The output must preserve the original question, use legible high-contrast handwriting or clean typesetting, remain in Chinese, and present tutor-like structure including Given, Analysis, Derivation, and Result. This transforms seemingly standard physics problem into much more demanding multimodal task. Experimental Setup. The case revolves around classic inclined-plane electromagnetic induction problem: conducting rod and U-shaped frame slide on smooth slope, enter and leave magnetic field at different moments, and are coupled through induced current, Ampere force, gravity, and friction. Unlike text-only physics QA, the model must jointly process three types of information: Dense document OCR: The problem statement is embedded in photographed or scanned exam page with Chinese text, inline formulas, and geometric diagram. Diagram grounding: Variables such as CD, EF , L, s0, s1, α, and the direction of the magnetic field must be aligned with the figure rather than treated as independent text tokens. Layout-aware response generation: The derivation must be inserted without obscuring the original question, and the rendered equations must remain readable and pedagogically structured. The target output therefore is not merely correct scalar answer. It is visually integrated worked solution. (a) Input: exam-style physics problem (b) Model output: solution rendered onto the image Figure 23 Physics problem solving as reasoning-conditioned document editing. The model is asked not only to solve the problem, but to write the derivation back onto the original visual artifact without obscuring the source text or diagram. This case therefore jointly tests OCR, diagram grounding, symbolic reasoning, layout planning, and text rendering. Generated by Nano Banana. Observed Behavior. As shown in Figure 23, the generated result is striking because it does more than decorate the image with red formulas. It appears to reconstruct plausible expert workflow: first deriving the rods entry velocity from energy conservation, then computing the induced current and Ampere force during the constant-velocity stage in the magnetic field, and finally solving for the rod mass, kinetic-friction coefficient, and the frames constant-velocity distance through coupled kinematic and force-balance analysis. The final image also demonstrates strong document-level awareness. It preserves the original question and diagram, writes the derivation into unused white space, keeps the explanation language consistent with the Chinese prompt, and uses contrasting red annotations to separate solution content from source content. Importantly, the final numerical results are not merely stylistic artifacts; they are mutually consistent under standard derivation, yielding FA = 0.18 N, 0.0352 kg, µ 0.111, and 0.666 m. What the Thinking Trace Reveals. The accompanying reasoning trace reveals much less smooth internal process than the polished output image suggests. Rather than following compact symbolic plan, the model repeatedly revisits the same subproblems: it re-analyzes the rod-frame velocity relation, reconsiders whether the relevant friction should be treated as kinetic or static, and repeatedly rebuilds the force-balance equations around the transition points at which the rod exits the magnetic field and the frame edge EF enters it. This repetitive self-correction is highly informative. It suggests that the model can eventually converge to coherent solution, but it does not maintain stable symbolic workspace in the way dedicated theorem prover or physics engine would. Instead, it appears to perform broad and somewhat redundant search over partially grounded hypotheses until numerically self-consistent chain of reasoning emerges.
